data.title: The Shard’s brickmaker collapses into administration
data.description:
The brickmaker that supplied The Shard has collapsed into administration amid a protracted slowdown in construction.

York Handmade Brick filed a notice of intention to appoint administrators on Friday, according to court records, and is represented by Addleshaw Goddard.

The manufacturer supplied 90,000 bespoke bricks to The Shard, which opened in 2013. The 87-storey skyscraper is predominantly glass and steel but has bricks on its first few floors, including a dramatic brickwork façade.

York bricks have also been used at London Bridge railway station, with 100,000 provided for the station’s overhaul ahead of its reopening in 2017.

Other projects it has been involved in include Magdalene College Library in Cambridge, which won the Royal Institute of British Architects’s Stirling Prize in 2022.

The company also supplied the bricks for the restoration of St Albans Cathedral in Hertfordshire, the oldest site of continuous Christian worship in Britain, the Westgate Oxford shopping centre redevelopment and York Racecourse.

Based at Alne, near Easingwold, the brickmaker has been running since 1988. It was founded by David Armitage, a fifth-generation brickmaker, and remains a family business. Guy Armitage, David’s son, is now the company’s managing director.

The company’s collapse into administration comes at a torrid time for construction suppliers and builders, as rising costs and a downturn in building heap pressure on the industry.

In a trading update on Tuesday, fellow brick manufacturer Michelmersh warned of a “notable slowdown” in the industry during the final quarter of the year.

Michelmersh said activity has been hit by a “challenging macro-economic outlook and the uncertainty of UK Budget policy announcements, which have adversely impacted both consumer sentiment and investment decision making”.

Builders were left disappointed by last week’s Budget, including Richard Beresford, chief executive of the National Federation of Builders, who said in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s statement that the “industry will remain in the mud”.

“Death by a thousand taxes has already killed off many businesses, with insolvency rates still high within the construction industry,” he said.

The construction sector shrank at its fastest pace in more than five years in October, according to the S&P Global UK Construction PMI index.

Brick makers are also among the industries struggling with high energy costs, and relief for manufacturers has been pushed back to 2027.

York Handmade Brick were contacted for comment, while Addleshaw Goddard declined to comment.

data.title: Gerry McCann, it’s press freedom not tabloid excess that is the issue today
data.description:
Gerry McCann’s anger at press intrusion following the disappearance of his daughter Madeleine is understandable. I’m sure it made every parent’s nightmare even more horrendous. But it isn’t a good argument for state regulation of the press, which he and others are calling for.

As a free speech advocate, I opposed the creation of a new, state-controlled press regulator when Sir Brian Leveson recommended it back in 2012. But the case for greater regulation of the press 13 years on is even weaker.

Gerry McCann describes the misery of having journalists camped out on his family’s doorstep for months after Madeleine’s disappearance and constantly being “monstered” by insensitive hacks. But how many of them worked for British newspapers likely to be within scope of a new regulator? Madeleine disappeared in Portugal and this was a story that attracted global interest, with journalists descending on the McCanns from all over the world. Tying the hands of just British journalists would not have ended the family’s torment.

And was press interest in the McCanns so inexcusable? There have been numerous cases of parents reporting their child missing, only for it to emerge that they were responsible for the child’s death. For instance, a French couple – Anne-Sophie Faucheur and Nicolas Willot – reported their five-year-old daughter Typhaine Taton missing in 2009, then, five months later, confessed to her murder. The McCanns are entirely innocent, of course, but were reporters so wrong to scrutinise their story?

Even if new regulation could somehow capture all journalists, not just those working for the British press, the media landscape has changed beyond all recognition in the past decade, with platforms like Facebook, X and Instagram becoming people’s main source of news.

Large social media providers are already subject to much tougher regulation than the print media, thanks to the draconian Online Safety Act, which gives Ofcom the power to fine them £18m or 10 per cent of their annual global turnover, whichever is the higher. Any “toughening up” of Ofcom’s regulation of these platforms, which some ministers have called for, would leave them with no choice but to geo-block the UK, leaving the British economy at a huge disadvantage next to its less regulated competitors.

The media is about to be transformed again by AI in ways we can only imagine. How would a new analogue regulator help manage the risks thrown up by that? Its only impact would be to hobble the print media, putting British newspapers out of business and depriving people of more reliable sources of news.

One of Leveson’s arguments for the creation of a state-managed regulator was that self-regulation had failed. In fact, the new independent regulator that replaced the Press Complaints Commission in the aftermath of the inquiry has proved to be effective – too effective, in the eyes of some, such as its recent decision to uphold a complaint against The Spectator for “misgendering” a trans-rights activist. An external review by Sir Bill Jeffrey published in April 2023 described Ipso as “well led and managed” and said its “supportive, but challenging engagement to improve standards” is “exactly what an effective regulator should be doing”.

But perhaps the most misguided thing about the call for tougher press regulation is that our print media is already hog-tied by laws and regulation fettering its freedom. Repeated court judgments in privacy cases have gone against newspapers, with complainants citing Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights as a justification for keeping their affairs secret even when there’s a clear public interest defence.

Since the Free Speech Union was set up in 2020, we’ve had to come to the defence of numerous journalists, including those investigated by the police for stirring up racial hatred because of interviews they’ve done with controversial figures and those stopped at ports of entry under Schedule 7 of the Terrorism Act.

Our most recent case, currently ongoing, involves a senior politician in Guernsey who has been arrested for “harassment” because he passed details to the British press about an expert witness in the Lucy Letby trial and raised doubts about her professional credentials in parliament.

If parliamentary privilege no longer protects politicians from being placed under investigation by the police for speech offences, things really have reached a pretty pass.

As JD Vance pointed out yesterday in response to the EU’s attempt to fine Elon Musk €120m (£105m), free speech is in crisis across Europe, particularly the UK. This is the time to do more to protect it, not erode it even further.

Lord Young of Acton is the director of the Free Speech Union

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”

data.title: Newspapers have changed – they don’t need further regulation
data.description:
Gerry McCann has called for new press regulation to prevent ordeals such as his family endured when their daughter was abducted in 2007. Yet the media world has changed beyond recognition since then. The Independent Press Standards Organisation (Ipso), introduced after the Leveson Inquiry in 2012, now regulates newspapers. A great deal of energy is devoted to compliance and complaints. The intrusive tabloid excesses of the last century are no longer an issue.

What has emerged as a serious problem is the unreliability of social media. Whereas the press is accountable to Ipso, social media providers are accountable to nobody. The result is that rumour, unfiltered by any editorial process, can spread online like wildfire.

The threat to ordinary citizens now comes, not from the press, but from social media influencers who care more about volume than veracity. A new Leveson Inquiry would do nothing for families like the McCanns.

However, a more draconian, state-controlled system of press regulation could do a lot of harm to freedom of speech and expression. The strength of Ipso lies in its independence from government. Britain, the first country to enjoy freedom of the press, should champion its importance – we certainly do not need further restrictions. Consider how this newspaper has held politicians to account, from the 2009 MPs’ expenses scandal to this year’s investigation of misconduct by ministers and officials, from Angela Rayner to Lord Mandelson. In countries where the state controls press regulation, such scandals are very rarely exposed. For reliable news and investigative reporting, nothing beats a press that is vigorous, viable and, above all, free.

data.title: Stock market history signals a reckoning for today’s AI spending surge
data.description:
Questor is The Telegraph’s stockpicking column, helping you decode the markets and offering insights on where to invest for the past six decades.

The debate over whether the artificial intelligence (AI) spending boom is primed for a bust continues to rumble.

On the one hand, Anthropic and OpenAI, the ChatGPT owner, are reportedly preparing themselves for stock market flotations with price tags in the hundreds of billions – if not trillions – of dollars.

On the other, Apple is the only one of the so-called Magnificent Seven whose shares are trading at a new all-time high as 2025 draws to a close – and it seems to be spending the least of any of them on AI.

In this context, a number of market shrewdies continue to point this column toward the history books and areas where investment is modest, even below historic averages, in the view that any upside surprise in demand will meet limited supply growth.

The net result could be much stronger prices, profits and cash flow than expected for producers. In this respect, the debate over oil is no less fierce than it is over AI.

Boom and bust

Many a stock market boom, from American railroads in the 19th century to broadband and 3G mobile telecoms equipment in the late 20th, has been fuelled by substantial capital investment in the latest technology and hopes for the associated productivity benefits.

The strong performance of shares in AI-related companies feels similar – but that raises the risk that the endgame may be the same. Capacity is built too quickly, demand is slow to catch up, profits disappoint and inevitably, spending is slashed.

Previously copious cash flow is already drying up at some of the biggest AI spenders, with the result that they are turning to debt at best – or worse, more opaque means of financing their investments through an interlocking web of supply agreements.

Even Nvidia’s balance sheet is showing strain as inventory and trade receivables rise faster than sales while growth in cash flow lags the rate of increase in revenues and profit.

These may not be red flags but they certainly have a pinkish hue – and investors are paying attention. Using the S&P Global 1200 indices as a benchmark, information technology is no longer the best-performing sector of 2025 to date, ranking third behind communication services and utilities.

This is a bit deceptive in some ways.

The biggest stocks in communication services by index weighting are Alphabet, Meta Platforms and Tencent, while utilities are being boosted as investors seek out providers of the energy that powers and cools the data centres and servers needed for the computing and inference capabilities of the large language models that in turn underpin AI.

All of this brings into relief once more the lopsided nature of the US – even global – stock markets. Technology and AI-related stocks dominate, often in unexpected ways.

Meanwhile, a poor second-half showing from sectors such as financials, industrials and real estate suggests there is some lingering disquiet over the global economic outlook, especially as more defensive areas such as healthcare and energy are doing a little better.

Power play

Energy’s gentle rally is surprising in the context of weak oil and gas prices – as well as hopes for peace in both the Middle East and Ukraine, where settlements could ease worries over the supply of hydrocarbons and perhaps lessen concerns over the importance of long-term independence.

A longer perspective than six months suggests that energy stocks are still in the doldrums.

The stock market capitalisation of the S&P Global 1200 Energy sector is just 3.3pc of that of the overall S&P Global 1200 index. That is not far from 2020’s Covid and lockdown-inspired low of 2.5pc in 2020 and well below the long-term average of 7.7pc, let alone 2007’s peak of 13.8pc.

This lack of interest reflects the view that renewables will take the lead in energy provision and also the risk that governments will continue to apply lofty tax rates if oil and gas producers’ profits stay higher for longer than expected.

The oil majors are getting the message. Their restrained capital expenditure budgets are a stark contrast to those of the AI hyperscalers.

Such relatively modest spending also suggests that supply of hydrocarbons will only grow slowly and that it will take time to bring on new fields, even if demand for energy worldwide continues to increase as the globe grapples with how best to manage the transition toward renewables.

This could yet lead to more interest in hydrocarbon assets and higher commodity prices, especially if sticky inflation or ongoing debt accumulation by Western governments persuade investors to trust “hard” assets more than “paper promises” such as cash and bonds.

data.title: Plans for Britain’s biggest Muslim cemetery revived by Issa brothers
data.description:
The billionaire Issa brothers have revived plans to build Britain’s biggest Muslim cemetery despite local opposition.

Mohsin and Zuber Issa, best known for their £7bn takeover of Asda, have been trying for the past four years to build the giant graveyard near Oswaldtwistle in Lancashire – a village with a population of 10,815 people.

The Memorial Garden, as it will be known, would surpass the Gardens of Peace in east London as the UK’s largest Muslim cemetery if built, and include a funeral parlour and prayer halls.

It has been twice rejected by local officials. The Issa brothers had withdrawn their plans in October after planning bosses said it would have a “harmful effect on [the] appearance of the area”.

Officers also claimed that the cemetery would pose an “unacceptable risk of pollution of groundwater”.

However, the Issas have now said they remain “committed” to the project after dismissing an application by campaigners to use the site for a nature reserve.

Zak Khan, a local Conservative councillor, and Vinette Davitt, a community activist, said they had written to the Issa Foundation – the brothers’ charity – suggesting they instead convert the land into a “network of enhanced habitats and attractive landscapes”.

However, a spokesman for the foundation said it would instead reapply to build the graveyard.

The charity said it had invited Mr Khan to meet to discuss his proposal, “but this offer was declined due to community concerns about the cemetery project”.

It added: “We remain of the view that our proposal respectfully protects the land from built development while equally offering environmental and community benefits. In the meantime, until a scheme with broad stakeholder support can be progressed, we will continue to farm the land and proactively maintain the site.”

The cemetery was seen as part of a wider move to grow their influence in Lancashire, including building a mosque with 95ft-high minarets and a £3m dental surgery.

The Issa brothers, who made their multibillion-pound fortune through growing petrol forecourt empire EG Group, are also building a series of mega mansions close to where they grew up in the Brookhouse area.

However, the cemetery project has been hit by a series of setbacks, with a local Tory councillor claiming that more than 10,000 residents are in opposition to the scheme.

Last year, they submitted a scaled-down planning application for the project - changing it from 35,000 burial plots to 12,250 and reducing the number of car parking spaces to 387 spaces, from more than 660 previously.

However, the project has continued to face opposition, with campaigners claiming it remains “huge” and takes up “over half of the green belt” in the area.

The Issa Foundation said its advisers had recommended that it continues to work on the cemetery plans, adding: “We have consistently prioritised high-quality landscaping, respecting public access, and celebrating the natural character of Oswaldtwistle and the surrounding area.”

data.title: Rightmove under pressure as activist investor takes £250m stake
data.description:
An activist investor has bought a near £250m stake in Rightmove, piling fresh pressure on the embattled property giant.

London-based Independent Franchise Partners (IFP) has built up a 5.8pc stake in the house-buying portal in recent weeks, becoming Rightmove’s third-biggest shareholder.

The investment was made after the company warned its profit growth will slow next year because of investment in AI. That announcement wiped £1bn off Rightmove’s market value.

IFP has in the past led activist campaigns against the likes of Japanese brewer Kirin – unsuccessfully pushing it to spin-off its non-beer assets – and is preparing to push for change at cosmetics company Shiseido.

The investment group is also one of the largest independent shareholders in Rupert Murdoch’s News Corp and Fox Corp. IFP publicly spoke out against plans for the two to recombine in 2022.

Founded in 2009 by a group of former Morgan Stanley employees, IFP has around $22bn (£16bn) in assets under management. It describes its approach as one that focuses on generating “attractive long-term returns” for clients “with a strong capital preservation bias”. IFP declined to comment.

IFP’s link to the Murdoch media empire is likely to reignite speculation that Rightmove could be a takeover target. Last year the property website rejected a £6.2bn takeover approach from REA Group, an Australian online property company owned by Mr Murdoch’s News Corp.

At the time it rejected the bid, Rightmove argued that it “materially” undervalued the business, pointing to “long-term growth and returns”.

But the £4bn company is now reeling from a dramatic slump in share prices, with stocks plunging by just over 30pc in the past six months.

Anthony Codling, an analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said: “If nothing changes with the share price, you could understand why [investors] might think [bids would return].”

Shares in Rightmove rose 1.8pc following The Telegraph’s report.

Rightmove’s grip on Britain’s property market makes it an attractive asset. It is the biggest property listings website in the UK by site traffic and property numbers, garnering more than 80pc of all time spent by consumers on property portals. The FTSE 100 company advertises one million UK homes every month.

However, investors have lately become concerned about the potential threat posed by AI. Chatbots could reinvent the way people hunt for houses, allowing people to ask for tailored listings.

Rightmove sought to counter the threat by unveiling plans to spend £60m on AI last month, including launching automated valuations, new conversational searches and visualisation tools. Johan Svanstrom, its chief executive, said AI was becoming “absolutely central” to its strategy.

But the announcement sent shares in the company tumbling after Rightmove said the investment would take three years to pay off, affecting profits until 2028.

The heavy spending comes against a backdrop of a subdued property market, with buyers and sellers reluctant to commit ahead of last month’s Budget and now facing uncertainty ahead of the introduction of a new council tax surcharge in 2028.

Rightmove also faces the threat of a £1bn lawsuit on behalf of estate agents who claim it abused its market dominance to charge “unfair” prices, backed by the litigation funding arm of US activist Elliott. The property portal has said it is “confident in the value we provide to our partners”.

Since August, Rightmove has lost £2.3bn of value. Giles Thorne, of Jefferies, said: “The more any share price goes down, by definition, the higher the probability an activist comes in.

“It’s just the rules of the game because they’ll see grounds for management to take some kind of strategic action that would push the share price up.”

Mr Codling said: “I would argue Rightmove’s management should focus just on running the business to the best of their ability and let the share price take care of itself.”

Rightmove has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Rail boss to exit after ghost train humiliation
data.description:
The chief executive of Britain’s rail regulator has said he will stand down, just days after an embarrassing climbdown over plans to run a high-speed service from Manchester to London without any passengers.

John Larkinson will leave the Office of Rail and Road (ORR) in April after more than 20 years at the regulator, the last seven of them as chief executive.

The announcement comes after Mr Larkinson was forced to concede defeat in a row over the 7am Avanti service from Manchester to London, which the ORR had insisted should run empty.

The regulator argued that the move was necessary to guarantee the train’s availability in the capital for morning services to the North.

Ministers intervened to compel Mr Larkinson to overturn the decision and open the train up to passengers following a public outcry.

While the ORR said Mr Larkinson had informed Declan Collier, the ORR chairman, of his plans to retire a month ago, the timing of the announcement risks stoking speculation about a widening rift between the regulator and the Department for Transport (DfT).

Days before the controversy over the Manchester service, Mr Larkinson and Stephanie Tobyn, the ORR’s strategy director, laid out their concerns about the Government’s rail nationalisation plan.

They warned the House of Commons Transport Committee late last month that the ORR would no longer be the ultimate decision-maker over who should have access to scarce operating routes on the busiest railway lines.

Instead, such rulings will be taken by Great British Railways (GBR) and the DfT.

It is understood that senior staff were informed only yesterday of Mr Larkinson’s exit. He first joined the ORR’s predecessor, the Strategic Rail Authority, in 2000.

The ORR said it would “now start the process of recruiting a successor”.

Reason for stepping down

Mr Larkinson said in an ORR statement that there was “never a perfect time to move on” while indicating that his decision had been influenced by the imminent arrival of GBR.

He said: “Most of my tenure as chief executive has been against a backdrop of rail reform and I feel that the introduction of the Railways Bill into Parliament is an appropriate time to depart.”

He added that “as an independent and expert body with a strong commitment to transparency, the ORR has played a crucial role in the development of the rail and road sectors” and that it should “continue to do so into the future”.

The ORR reinstated the Manchester service under pressure from Peter Hendy, the Rail Minister, and Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, who said “passengers were rightly baffled” by the so-called ghost train.

She added that nationalisation “will ensure problems like these are eliminated”.

Despite its climbdown, the regulator has continued to insist that the service posed a risk to smooth operations on Europe’s busiest intercity route.

A spokesman said: “While we believe the running of this 7am service could have a negative impact on reliability and punctuality of services on the west coast mainline, we understand the importance of this service and we’ll support its continued operation.”

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: HSBC bans Christmas jumpers over fears they could trigger customers
data.description:
HSBC has banned staff from wearing Christmas jumpers over fears they could upset some customers.

The bank has told its employees not to wear the festive items this year after some customers complained about the professionalism of the outfits.

It added that its branches were spaces where customers received support with bereavement and financial difficulty, services that require “professionalism and sensitivity”.

According to The Sun, which first reported the news, there were also concerns that it would add unnecessary complications if staff had to take off a Christmas jumper before a serious conversation.

Last year, employees at one branch were photographed wearing festive knitwear, including one featuring the Grinch in a Santa hat alongside the phrase “Merry whatever”.

It is understood that the decision had nothing to do with cultural sensitivities. A HSBC spokesman said: “We are encouraging colleagues to celebrate Christmas in branches in lots of different ways, including decorating branches, team celebrations and festive lunches.

“However, it’s important to also remember that branches are spaces where customers receive a range of specialist services that require professionalism and sensitivity. This year, we have asked colleagues in customer-facing roles not to wear Christmas jumpers to reflect this.”

While festive jumpers have been banned at HSBC, the Church of England recently gave its blessing to even the most lurid Christmas sweaters.

Nicholas Papadopulos, the Dean of Salisbury, wrote in a booklet produced by Church House Publishing: “You see, Christmas jumpers are tasteless. And here’s the thing. God is tasteless too. That’s actually the heart of the good news. God is without a shred of cultural or aesthetic judgment. That must be so.”

Mr Papadopulos added that God is also a “knitter” who is thanked for “carefully weaving us into being”, adding: “God has no taste. God does not discriminate. God does not exclude. So wear that jumper with pride.”

In 2023, HSBC shifted to more casual uniforms for its staff. These included a new range of jumpsuits, chinos and jeans, which it said would mirror the “more casual new look of the banks’ branches”.

The bank has 327 branches across the country. Last year, it said visits had fallen by 65pc over the previous five years. Since 2015, HSBC has closed 743 outlets. However, it has committed to announcing no new branch closures until “at least” 2026.

Employees from branches of other banks, including NatWest and Metro Bank, have been photographed wearing Christmas jumpers this year.

It is unclear whether HSBC’s ban will also be enforced on Christmas Jumper Day, an annual fundraising campaign organised by Save the Children, where people are encouraged to raise funds for the charity by wearing a Christmas jumper to work.

Save the Children launched the campaign in 2012, with more than £40m has been raised since then. The charity estimates that more than 17 million people have taken part since it was first started.

It is scheduled to take place on Dec 11 this year. Last year, the campaign raised £2.8m.

Celebrities including Olly Murs, Myleene Klass and Alison Hammond have all supported the campaign in the past.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Channel Tunnel owner blames tax rises as it scraps UK investment
data.description:
The owner of the Channel Tunnel has scrapped all UK investment following the Government’s decision to burden it with tens of millions of pounds in extra taxes.

Eurotunnel said plans to increase its business rates bill by almost 200pc were “unjustified and confiscatory in nature”.

As a result, it said it was suspending plans to develop a freight terminal in Barking, east London, which would have allowed it to run dedicated freight services from France and free up capacity on passenger routes.

The Valuation Office Agency (VOA), which provides the valuations across England and Wales that underpin property taxes, has informed the company that it plans to almost triple the rateable value of its properties from next year.

This would increase Eurotunnel’s annual business rates bill from £22m to £65m in the coming years, although the increase would be phased in, meaning its bill in 2026 will initially jump to £35m.

The VOA has been tasked with overhauling business rates by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, as she wants companies with bigger premises to pay more in levies to help reduce costs for smaller sites.

“Eurotunnel expresses its deep disagreement with this planned increase and considers it is unjustified and confiscatory in nature, and that it threatens Eurotunnel’s investment capacity in Great Britain,” the company said on Thursday.

“Eurotunnel will continue its efforts to negotiate with the VOA and its supervisory authorities until the final valuation decision, expected at the end of March 2026.”

Yann Leriche, the chief executive, told the Financial Times that the move “makes all of our investments loss-making, so we won’t be making any more investments”.

He added: “As of today, we have frozen our investment in rail assets in the UK.”

He said the suspended investment amounted to £15m, while Eurotunnel suggested it could challenge the decision.

Cancelling plans to boost freight through the Channel Tunnel would conflict with the Government’s hopes of taking cargo off the roads by boosting rail.

“Eurotunnel believes that it is currently unduly penalised compared to its competitors whose activities are more carbon-intensive, less-taxed, and some of which are deviating from the social models applicable in France and Great Britain,” the company said.

Earlier this week, the boss of airline Jet2 complained that the Government was treating the travel industry like a “cash cow”.

This month, electric vehicle charging companies wrote to the Treasury warning that plans to introduce business rates on chargers’ parking bays would hurt the adoption of EVs.

A VOA spokesman said: “The VOA is responsible for non-domestic valuations which reflect changes in the property market. All valuations are carried out by experienced professionals in accordance with industry best practice and legal requirements. The VOA does not determine business rates and next year’s liability has not yet been confirmed.

“We have engaged with Getlink and their advisers on multiple occasions over the past 18 months to discuss their valuation and fully explain our approach. These discussions remain ongoing and we are committed to continuing constructive engagement.

“Ratepayers have the right to formally challenge their valuation with the VOA if they believe it is inaccurate. Should they remain dissatisfied with our response, they may appeal to the independent Valuation Tribunal.”

data.title: Heathrow to fine drivers £80 for overly long farewells
data.description:
People who take too long to bid farewell to loved ones at Heathrow face an £80 fine for overstaying their welcome.

A new 10-minute limit for unloading bags and wishing passengers a safe journey at Britain’s busiest airport will leave no time for drawn-out goodbyes.

The cap will be introduced for drivers at Heathrow’s forecourts from January as part of a shake-up that will push drop-off charges up from £6 to £7 in an inflation-busting 17pc increase.

Unlike most other major airports in the UK, Heathrow has not previously sought to limit the time people can spend dropping off friends, family and business colleagues.

However, hubs such as Gatwick and Luton allow over-stayers to pay by the minute rather than hitting them with a hefty fine.

Clive Wratten, the chief executive of the Business Travel Association, a lobby group, said the higher fees and strict time limit risk punishing passengers unnecessarily.

He said: “There’s next to no justification for this. These charges do nothing to improve the passenger experience or reliability of access to Heathrow. All it does is raise costs.”

Heathrow claimed that the 10-minute maximum stay, to be introduced across all of its terminal forecourts, would ease congestion and improve traffic flows.

It said: “This will enhance the customer experience for those using the terminal drop off as it is intended.”

It said 95pc of drop-offs take less than 10 minutes and that the time limit is expected to have a “minimal impact”.

A spokesman added: “Most people just drop off and go, they don’t hang around. It’s very busy, and we need to move people on. There just isn’t space in those terminal forecourts.”

One response to the scheme, however, could be for drivers who need more than 10 minutes to circle round and return to the drop-off area, accruing a £14 fee rather than the £80 fine, potentially adding to congestion.

Heathrow said the £7 basic charge – also backed up by an £80 fine for non-payment, reduced to £40 if settled within 14 days – brings it into line with other airports and is aimed partly at encouraging “options for travel that have a lower environmental impact”.

While Gatwick already charges £7 for the first 10 minutes on its forecourts, people can stay for longer at a cost of £1 per minute, up to a maximum of 30 minutes and £27.

Luton operates a similar system, charging £7 for 10 minutes and £1 per minute thereafter, again with a maximum stay of 30 minutes.

Manchester and Stansted airports have somewhat lower basic charges but a flat £25 rate for people who extend their time in the drop-off areas.

Heathrow said blue-badge holders will be exempt from the maximum stay limit and will still be able to use drop-off zones for free, so long as they register their trip.

data.title: Ozempic maker suffers boardroom exodus after row with shareholder
data.description:
The wealthy foundation behind Ozempic maker Novo Nordisk has seized control of the company in a boardroom coup after a row over the struggling group’s turnaround.

On Monday, the pharmaceutical giant said chairman Helge Lund and six independent directors would resign and be replaced by a group hand-picked by its biggest shareholder, the Novo Nordisk Foundation.

The drugmaker, which makes the anti-obesity jab Wegovy and Ozempic, said the decision came after a disagreement between the board and the foundation over a plan to rejuvenate the Danish company’s flagging fortunes.

The Novo Nordisk Foundation is the wealthiest charity in the world with £123bn in assets and the company’s largest shareholder, controlling 77pc of Novo.

The foundation has nominated five new directors to be appointed at an emergency meeting next month and a sixth to be appointed next year.

Lars Rebien Sørensen, the former chief executive of Novo, has been nominated to replace Mr Lund as chairman. Mr Lund recently left his position chairing BP after a disastrous attempt to focus the company on green energy.

Novo’s exodus amounts to the entire board, besides the foundation’s own investment boss and the four members elected by employees.

The rift between the company’s top shareholders and its board came to the fore after a slump in Novo’s performance.

The company makes the diabetes drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, which are used for weight loss. Both contain the ingredient semaglutide, which stimulates weight loss by making people feel full.

The initial success of the drugs led Novo to become Europe’s most valuable company, but shares have fallen by two thirds as it has faced competition from Eli Lilly, which makes rival drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Novo’s own successor to Wegovy disappointed in weight loss trials, while the Trump administration has put pressure on the company to cut prices.

“Following dialogue with the Novo Nordisk Foundation regarding the future composition of the board of directors, it has not been possible to reach a common understanding,” Novo said.

“The board proposed a renewal focusing on [the] addition of select, new competencies while also maintaining continuity, whereas the board of the foundation wanted a more extensive reconfiguration.”

It said the new board’s first priority would be “implementing its transformation plans and regaining the company’s growth momentum”.

Mr Sørensen said: “Given the fast-moving environment in which Novo Nordisk operates, we believe that it is in the best interest of the company and its shareholders to implement a board renewal as soon as possible, rather than wait until the ordinary annual general meeting in March next year.”

He said the foundation fully supported the company’s new chief executive, Mike Doustdar, who replaced ousted boss Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen earlier this year, as well as recent plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

Novo’s shares fell by 2.4pc after the announcement.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Moving house? Call your energy supplier or you could soon be cut off
data.description:
New homeowners who fail to contact their energy supplier when moving into a property face having their gas and electricity cut off after a fortnight.

Industry regulator Ofgem unveiled plans on Thursday to prevent people from running up unpaid debts when they move house, as part of efforts to tackle bill-dodgers.

Currently when new tenants move in, accounts are switched to an anonymous “occupier” status and bills build up until the new homeowners contact the supplier.

Under the new plan, energy meters will be switched to “prepayment” mode with a £30 credit when the previous resident moves out – enough to last roughly two weeks. If no contact is made after this, the supplies will be cut off.

Ofgem warned the proposed changes were “significant” and would need to be enacted carefully because there was “a long-established expectation that energy supply will be uninterrupted when occupiers move into a new property”.

On average, new homeowners take 70 days to contact their energy supplier.

The changes are part of reforms aimed at reducing the £4.4bn debt owed by households to suppliers, which has nearly tripled over the past five years.

Reform ‘long overdue’

The cost of managing or writing off these debts is spread across all bill-payers and adds £52 per year to the typical household bill.

It is estimated that debts incurred after home moves account for a third of the total.

Charlotte Friel of Ofgem said the proposed changes “aim to stop debt in its tracks, tackling today’s problem and preventing tomorrow’s”.

“Major reform of the home-move process is overdue,” she said. “These proposed changes will benefit all energy consumers and bring us in line with other comparable markets.”

At the moment, gas and power supplies continue to flow into homes even when the occupier is unknown, allowing people to incur significant debt.

This differs from other services such as broadband, where consumers are responsible for ensuring they are connected.

In consultation documents, Ofgem said debt built up following home moves accounted for between 20pc and 40pc of household debt overall, and this proportion had remained steady over the years.

That suggested the problem was at least partly “driven by behavioural factors rather than affordability”, the watchdog argued.

Whereas broadband customers had a “strong incentive” to contact their supplier to access internet services, the lack of consequences for not contacting energy providers “takes away the incentive” to do so.

“If that incentive doesn’t exist at the key trigger point of the home move, then later prompts, such as letters from suppliers, may have less impact,” Ofgem added.

“Some consumers might be remiss about contacting a supplier, but are able to pay their bills.

“Those consumers may nevertheless be keen to avoid accumulating debt, and incentives to contact a supplier at an early stage may be welcomed.”

But campaign group National Energy Action said the reform could “bring its own issues”.

Peter Smith, the group’s head of policy, said: “The aim is to encourage people moving house to prioritise signing up to a supplier, but we regularly see prepayment customers self-disconnect.

“Moving home can be stressful enough without vulnerable customers struggling with no heating and hot water when they first move in or remaining on a prepayment tariff even if it isn’t right for them.”

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Scotland’s key carbon capture project faces collapse in new blow to Miliband
data.description:
Scotland’s flagship carbon capture project is at risk of collapse after its main backer announced plans to exit the scheme.

The Acorn project, based in Peterhead, Aberdeenshire, is being built to capture CO2 from heavy industry and then bury it under the North Sea.

But the driving force behind the project, energy group Storegga, said on Thursday it planned to sell its stake in the scheme, delivering a major blow to the net zero push spearheaded by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

The project had already secured significant political backing after Rachel Reeves,the Chancellor, offered the scheme £200m in development funding in her June spending review.

Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, Ms Reeves and Mr Miliband have all visited Peterhead’s St Fergus gas terminal, where Acorn is based, to champion Labour’s green policies.

Storegga said it would sell its Acorn stake following a “strategic review of its business, capital requirements and future structure”.

It said: “As part of this, we are progressing a structured sales process for our portfolio of assets, including the sale of our interest in the Acorn CCS project.

“With Acorn approaching a more capital-intensive phase, and with both the UK and Scottish Governments signalling the importance of its timely delivery, we have concluded that a new long-term owner would be better placed to take the project forward.”

Storegga is owned by group of international investors, including Singapore’s sovereign wealth fund GIC, Macquarie, Adnoc, M&G, Mitsui and Snam.

Acorn is also backed by Shell UK and Harbour Energy – both of which have recently decided to minimise their involvement in UK waters.

Shell this week hived off almost all its UK offshore assets into a new company, Adura, while Harbour announced 100 more job cuts taking the total to 700 in three years.

Storegga’s move also follows Ms Reeves’s refusal in last week’s Budget to reduce the so-called windfall tax, which has raised levies on UK oil and gas profits to 78pc.

The future involvement of Shell and Harbour in the Acorn plan remains uncertain.

Shell this week moved almost all its UK offshore assets into a new company, Adura, but the Acorn project is still part of Shell.

Harbour is also a key partner in the Viking carbon capture project on the Humber as well as Acorn.

It has previously warned it may quit both schemes because the windfall tax has forced it to make such deep cuts in its workforce. Asked if Harbour remained committed to Acorn a company spokesman refused to comment.

The Acorn project’s aim is to build infrastructure to collect CO2 from Scotland’s remaining heavy industry, including power stations and chemical works.

Initially this would involve collecting CO2 from the St Fergus plant which is a gas terminal processing up to half of the UK’s offshore production of gas.

More would come from the Ineos chemical works at Grangemouth, with pipes eventually connecting to more heavy industry across Scotland’s central belt.

The waste gas would then be compressed into a liquid and pumped offshore for burial in rock formations deep beneath the seabed.

However, critics have warned that there may not be enough CO2 needing disposal to justify the investments needed for Acorn to start operations.

Stephen Flynn, the SNP’s Westminster leader, said Mr Miliband should keep Acorn alive by forcing his GB Energy quango to invest in the scheme.

He said: “This is a critical moment for jobs and the economy. We have fought for years for funding for this project because of its local, national and international significance and just as the Labour Party has instructed GB Energy to stick solar panels on school roofs in England, so too must they now instruct it to offer a backstop to prevent this project from collapsing.”

Sir Ian Wood, the Scottish billionaire who made a fortune from the North Sea while running the John Wood Group, called on the UK and Scottish governments to save the project.

“The Acorn project is a cornerstone of Scotland’s energy transition ... It is now incumbent on the PM to act decisively and end the existing windfall tax – especially as [falling oil prices] mean there is no windfall.”

A spokesman for the UK Government said: “This commercial decision by Storegga Ltd has no bearing on the commercial viability of the Acorn CCS project.

“We remain committed to the Acorn CCS project and are working closely with the Scottish Government and industry to secure a buyer and realise the site’s potential to create thousands of jobs and drive industrial renewal in Scotland.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Larry Ellison’s Oracle plunges as AI drive fails to pay off
data.description:
More than $100bn (£75bn) has been wiped off the value of Larry Ellison’s cloud computing giant Oracle over fears that his bet on AI is failing to pay off.

Shares in Oracle fell by as much as 16.5pc when trading began on Wall Street on Thursday. The slump came after the company committed more investment into AI data centres, even as quarterly profit and revenue forecasts missed analysts’ expectations.

The results were released after markets closed on Wednesday and rattled tech stocks around the world, with Japan’s Nikkei down nearly 1pc.

The FTSE 100 and European stocks also fell, while tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite in the US dropped by around 1pc.

Oracle chairman Mr Ellison, a close ally of Donald Trump, has pushed his company into making massive infrastructure investments in an effort to capitalise on the promise of the AI revolution.

The company’s market valuation soared to nearly $1tn in September after it announced a $300bn deal to provide computing power to OpenAI, the developer of ChatGPT.

However, its shares have plunged by a third since then, wiping off more than $300bn in value amid doubts about the speed at which its investment will become profitable.

Tech stocks have also been rocked by fears of an AI bubble, fuelled by a series of so-called circular deals between some of the world’s biggest tech companies, including Oracle, Nvidia and OpenAI.

Meanwhile, Oracle has faced scrutiny over the amount of debt it has used to fund its AI investments.

The price of its credit-default swaps (CDS), financial instruments used to guard against potential non-payment of debts, has spiked in recent weeks to its highest level in three years.

Jacob Bourne, an analyst at Emarketer, said: “Oracle faces its own mounting scrutiny over a debt-fuelled data centre build-out and concentration risk amid questions over the outcome of AI spending uncertainty.

“This revenue miss will likely exacerbate concerns among already cautious investors about its OpenAI deal and its aggressive AI spending.”

The disappointing results from Oracle brought an end to a boost brought about by the US Federal Reserve’s decision to cut interest rates for the third time in a row.

SoftBank Group, a partner with Oracle on the US Stargate data centre project, sank by 7.5pc, dragging down tech stocks in Tokyo.

Shares in Hong Kong, Shanghai, Seoul, Taipei and Bangkok were also down.

“Even as investors were reassured by the Fed’s latest rate cut, familiar concerns about AI are still very much top of mind right now,” said Deutsche Bank analyst Jim Reid.

The results come at a delicate time for the Ellison family.

Larry’s son, David Ellison, is chairman and chief executive of Paramount, which has launched a hostile takeover bid for Warner Bros.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Businessman admits scamming airlines with fake parts
data.description:
An engineering boss has admitted supplying fake parts to airlines such as Ryanair in a scandal that grounded hundreds of planes around the world.

Jose Alejandro Zamora Yrala, the chief executive of UK-based AOG Technics, on Monday pleaded guilty to selling counterfeit aircraft engine parts in a scam that the Serious Fraud Office (SFO) said risked public safety on a global scale.

Mr Yrala, 37, of Virginia Water in Surrey, was granted conditional bail at Southwark Crown Court to appear for his sentencing hearing at the same court on Feb 23.

Between 2019 and 2023, the SFO said Mr Yrala defrauded customers by selling them bogus components that were eventually fitted into the world’s most widely used passenger aircraft engine powering the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320.

AOG acted as a broker for engine parts, acquiring older models and then falsifying official safety documents to pass them off as new or reconditioned.

Thousands of flights were delayed after the scandal emerged in August 2023 as airlines were forced to ground jets and examine their engines.

The deception began to unravel when a technician at a Portuguese repair centre noticed a discrepancy with a “new” part supplied by AOG, which appeared to be old.

After warnings to British, American and European regulators, Ryanair, Tui, Virgin Australia and nearly every major US carrier had to ground planes to weed out the bogus components.

Emma Luxton, the SFO’s director of operations, said: “This significant and audacious fraud threatened trust in the aviation industry and risked public safety on a global scale.

“I’m extremely proud that we’ve acted swiftly to secure this conviction and wish to thank law enforcement partners in Portugal for their speedy and critical assistance.”

The supply of parts in aviation is done through a patchwork of thousands of small manufacturers, brokers and distributors.

Manufacturers and operators are heavily regulated and largely responsible for orchestrating the paper trail that follows every aviation part, from computers and fuselage panels to screw housings and washers.

Mr Yrala founded AOG in 2015 from a rented terraced house in Hove, West Sussex, before moving the business to London.

The SFO launched its investigation alongside Portuguese authorities in 2023 and the Portuguese case remains ongoing.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hotel tycoon attacks Heathrow boss over runway plans
data.description:
Hotel tycoon Surinder Arora has accused Heathrow bosses of “propagating myths” about his bid to build a third runway at Europe’s busiest airport.

Mr Arora said Heathrow had waged a campaign to undermine his proposals for a shorter, cheaper strip than the one proposed by the airport itself. He called on Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, to debate the rival plans.

The tycoon said Heathrow falsely claimed his scheme would mean the demolition of “a crazy number of houses” and require a motorway spur from the M4 to the airport to be lowered beneath the proposed runway, interfering with railway lines.

Mr Arora shot back and claimed the airport had failed to explain how its own longer runway would span the M25 without the rebuilding of many more road links that it had admitted to.

The tussle over the rival runway bids has turned personal days before ministers are due to decide which of the two to take forward as part of Labour’s plan to encourage long-term growth through big-ticket infrastructure projects.

Heathrow has proposed a 3,500-metre strip to the north west of the current runways. While it would lift capacity to 150 million passengers a year, it would cost £49bn to deliver and involve redirecting the M25 under the new runway.

Meanwhile, Mr Arora, who owns hotels around Heathrow, has proposed building a 2,800-metre runway with a price tag of no more than £25bn that would avoid the M25 and, he claims, accommodate almost as many flights as the airport’s own blueprint.

He said: “Our dear friends at Heathrow will always try to propagate myths and doubts about us. But when it comes to their own scheme, it’s smoke and mirrors.

“If Thomas Woldbye wants to stand up in public and have a debate, I’m more than happy to do that, because we’ve got nothing to hide.

“People said to me at the start, are you sure you want to do this? It’s David against Goliath. Heathrow are a big machine, they’ve got big shareholders and they’re going to try to blow you away. And that’s what they’ve been trying to do.”

The Department for Transport (DfT) is expected to announce its preferred option for the third runway by the end of the month. It is understood that it may do so before the Budget in order to give Chancellor Rachel Reeves the opportunity to champion the project.

As a decision looms, Heathrow revealed the chief executives of 13 suppliers and terminal retailers had written to Heidi Alexander, the transport secretary, expressing “full support” for its plans. The companies included retailers Burberry and Fortnum & Mason and builder Costain.

Mr Arora said Heathrow was “clutching onto straws” after seeing support for its runway plan, part of a £49.1bn expansion scheme, ebbing away.

Sean Doyle, the chief executive of British Airways, said last week his company, the biggest airline at Heathrow, was open to a shorter runway and said if the M25 could be avoided, it should be.

Mr Arora said: “They never thought they would be in this position, having to justify their existence. But the train has left the station. All their behaviour over the last decades and their abuse of their monopoly and lack of value for money for customers and airlines, all that is coming out.

“People are beginning to realise that the regulatory system is broken and that the track record of these guys is so bad. Everybody is fed up and saying enough is enough.

“If I was knocking on your door with that track record and saying I am the most expensive builder and you’re going to get crap service, because I’m not even in the top 20 in the world, wouldn’t you slam the door in my face?”

Carlton Brown, Arora Group’s finance chief, said he expected the decision on the runway to turn on whether or not the Government trusts Heathrow to build a strip over the M25.

He said: “There is only one real question: can you avoid the M25 or not? That is the problem the DfT now faces, because we have shown definitively you can do it.”

A Heathrow spokesman said its plans been closely scrutinised for a decade and previously won the support of Parliament, “so it’s hardly smoke and mirrors.”

He said the notion that Heathrow was delivering a poor service passenger “is not reflective of reality,” with the hub the most punctual in Europe and ranked 22nd out of more than 500 worldwide according to Skytrax passenger surveys.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
