data.title: Homeowners’ bathroom renovations ‘delaying new builds’
data.description:
Safety checks on bathroom renovations are delaying the construction of high-rise flats as building inspectors struggle to cope with paperwork, leading peers have warned.

On Thursday, a House of Lords committee review of the Building Safety Regulator (BSR) found that even simple projects, such as remodelling bathrooms or replacing appliances, in tower blocks must be scrutinised under the national building safety checks system.

It said the flood of safety checks was causing “unacceptable” delays by the BSR in approving new high-rise housing.

“It strains credulity that works such as bathroom renovations in higher-risk buildings are being subject to the scrutiny of the BSR’s multidisciplinary teams,” the report said.

“This ties up scarce skilled professionals on non-safety-critical applications and subjects leaseholders to significant delays and costs.”

Steve Evans, from the National House Building Council, also told peers that bathroom renovations were “tying up our most skilled and best registered building inspectors”, already in high demand for more important safety inspections.

The BSR was established in 2022 following the Grenfell Tower disaster to raise safety standards across Britain’s buildings, with the most rigorous inspections for blocks taller than 18 metres.

But the BSR has become notorious for delaying housing projects and the development of existing homes.

Relatively minor works, such as plumbing changes, require regulatory approval before work can proceed.

Baroness Taylor of Bolton, chairman of the Lords committee scrutinising the BSR, said: “The scale of the delays caused by the BSR has stretched far beyond the regulator’s statutory timelines for building control decisions. This is unacceptable.

“We welcome that the Government and the BSR are now acting to try and make practical improvements, but this will not address the anxiety and frustration that residents and companies have experienced.

“It does not improve safety to delay vital remediation and refurbishments, nor to deter the delivery of new housing in high-rise buildings.”

It comes amid a critical skills shortage for building inspectors, with experts pointing to an ageing workforce struggling to meet demand.

Costs are also high, with one contractor, Thomson Brothers, estimating that the average application fee was just over £6,000.

Residents installing an additional toilet in their bathroom, for example, pay the same hourly rate as a company building a block of flats.

Sam Richards, the chief executive of campaign group Britain Remade, said: “Subjecting bathroom renovations in high-rise buildings to oversight of a regulator is just nonsensical.

“We have also seen first-hand how it delays and adds cost to even the simplest home improvements. It is indefensible that replacing a rotting wooden window in a ground-floor flat can be tied up in red tape for months and cost thousands.

“Created in good faith after the Grenfell tragedy, the BSR now risks stopping London building at all. It is blocking thousands of homes across the capital, including 1,210 completed homes left empty because the regulator could not sign them off.”

The Lords committee also warned that the Government will miss its target to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament unless it takes more action to resolve the issue.

During the inquiry, some witnesses suggested that the BSR had not improved safety because of delays in critical repair work.

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “The Building Safety Regulator’s new leadership has immediately focused on speeding up decision-making, and we will work closely with the regulator to go further to reduce unnecessary delays without compromising safety.

“This includes recruiting 100 new staff by the end of this year and launching a new building acceleration package to overhaul the regulator’s performance.”

data.title: Starmer gets key details of North Sea windfall tax wrong
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer has been accused of “staggering ineptitude” after he apparently failed to grasp key details about his North Sea windfall tax.

Speaking on a visit to Scotland on Thursday, the Prime Minister claimed incorrectly that the tax on energy profits only kicked in when prices rose sufficiently to create a genuine windfall.

He said this was why Labour was keeping the 78pc tax in place until 2030 – despite the destruction of 1,000 jobs a month as energy companies quit the North Sea.

In fact, the “windfall tax” applies to all profits even if prices have dropped below levels last seen during the energy crisis – meaning there is no longer any windfall.

Asked why he had left the tax in place, Sir Keir stated apparently incorrectly: “As the name makes absolutely clear, this tax only kicks in in relation to particular levels of profit. So it only kicks in at a particular level, so that’s the trigger for it.”

In a separate interview, he also said: “On the windfall tax ... that’s a windfall tax on excessive profits, so it doesn’t kick in. It’s not an automatic tax. The tax only cuts in when profits get to a certain level.”

The blunder provoked fury from leaders of Scotland’s offshore industry.

Robin Allan of Brindex, a trade body for North Sea operators, said Sir Keir appeared “incapable of describing the policies his government have enacted”.

Andrew Bowie, the shadow Scotland secretary, accused the Prime Minister of “staggering ineptitude”.

The windfall tax is blamed for destroying the industry since its introduction by the Conservatives in 2022. When Labour won power last year, it increased overall levies from 75pc to 78pc and extended the windfall tax to 2030.

Daniel Slater, an analyst at investment bank Zeus Capital, said the PM should be forced to clarify his comments to win back support from the industry.

“This levy is continuing to do damage to the UK oil and gas sector, jobs across the supply chain and UK energy security,” he said.

“I think working people in the UK oil and gas industry would like to see the Prime Minister clarify his comments, to reassure the country that the Government does understand what it is doing.”

There is a mechanism to switch the tax off – but for that to happen, oil prices must average less than $76.12 and gas prices less than 59p per therm, both for a full six months.

Oil is well below its threshold but gas is set to stay well above the 59p level, meaning no relief from the levy is in sight.

It means multiple companies have announced cutbacks or departures from UK waters. Harbour Energy, the largest UK operator, has cut 700 of its 1,200 UK staff since the levy was introduced and moved almost all future investment abroad.

Bob Sanguinetti, who runs the Port of Aberdeen, said the windfall tax had been a disaster for his business with a 25pc drop in supplies going out to the offshore platforms through the port.

“This reflects the dramatic reduction in activity in the energy sector across the length and breadth of the country, with a direct impact on the nation’s ability to accelerate the transition to renewables and an increasing reliance on imports for the UK’s energy needs,” he said.

Mr Allan said: “Unfortunately, the Prime Minister evidently does not understand how the oil and gas industry is taxed in the UK. How can the industry expect to get fair treatment if the leader of the country is incapable of describing the policies his government have enacted?

“It is clear evidence that the Government has not properly understood the detail and implications of their policy on oil and gas.”

Russell Borthwick, the chief executive of the Aberdeen & Grampian Chamber of Commerce, said: “The problem is the windfalls came and went three years ago. The energy transition he promised is turning into an economic nightmare for energy communities.”

The Cabinet Office, Treasury and Department of Energy Security and Net Zero were all asked for comment.

data.title: Locals sleep in 1950s bus shelter to stop council demolishing it
data.description:
Residents of a seaside town are camping out in their “cherished” 1950s bus shelter to stop a council demolishing it.

Demonstrators in Sheringham, Norfolk, have been braving the cold weather to protect the structure from demolition since Dec 1.

Norfolk county council wants to knock down the brick shelter outside the North Norfolk Railway Station and replace it with a modern glass and steel “hub”.

But for residents, the shelter, which showcases a mural from local artist Colin Seal, is a “cherished” part of the town’s identity that needs to be saved.

The group are considering continuing their sit-in on Christmas Day.

Save Sheringham’s Historic Bus Shelter, a campaign group, said: “We’ve lost track of the number of people we’ve spoken to who have expressed their support in so many ways, from Sheringham and beyond. This is growing, day by day, hour by hour.

“We want as many people as possible to come down between those hours to stand and support us. As with everything we’ve done so far, this is to be peaceful, respectful and lawful.”

The impromptu protest began when the workmen were spotted arriving at the scene on Monday afternoon and police were on-scene.

Since then, protesters have camped out all day and all night in shifts in front of the shelter to foil the council’s plans of demolishing the structure.

However, despite their efforts, the county council has said that it will not be backing down.

Instead it has asked for the protesters to “leave immediately” and argued that disrupting the works by entering a construction site is “dangerous” for everyone.

The council also reissued its stance on the issue when asked by this paper if it still intended to go ahead with tearing down the structure.

Katie Hart set up the original petition to get the bus shelter listed and has camped out since 6.30pm on Tuesday night.

She said: “The police came to question us. We thought ‘Can we get in here without causing damage?’

“They said a complaint has been put in and the contractors accused us of breaking a clip to get in here.

“We want to keep it peaceful. We are not here to antagonise. We are here to make a point to the council that no one is listening. Our own MP is asking them to halt.”

Lisa Woods, the part-owner of The Sheringham Trawler restaurant, brought chips out to the demonstrators.

She said: “It is the nostalgia of Sheringham. I think the plans are the worst thing that a lot of people have heard.”

A spokesman for the council said: “The concerns raised by some local residents are fully noted and have been considered throughout the development of the project, with many suggestions being incorporated into the design.

“But retaining the existing shelter is not a viable option and disrupting the works by entering a construction site is dangerous for all concerned.

“We want these works to be delivered as safely and quickly as possible so everyone living and working in or visiting Sheringham can benefit from this new amenity and we ask that those involved leave the area immediately.”

Lib Dem MP tables motion

Steff Aquarone, the North Norfolk MP, stood with the protesters in support at the shelter on Thursday. He said on a social media post: “When we stand together, we can create change. Conservative-run Norfolk county council must engage and listen, instead of ploughing ahead with their bullyboy approach.”

Mr Aquarone has also tabled an “early day motion”, which has gained the support of nine other Lib Dems, in a bid to get the matter discussed in Parliament.

It calls on the county council to suspend its plans to allow for further “constructive discussion with the community” and said: “Sheringham is a town which is not to be messed with.”

Norfolk Police said: “Officers were called to Station Approach, Sheringham at 9.45am (Dec 3) to a report of criminal damage to fencing surrounding a bus shelter.

“Initial inquiries were conducted, and no crime has been recorded.”

data.title: Tom Kerridge: Reeves is about to double my property tax bill
data.description:
Tom Kerridge has said Labour’s tax increases make it harder to keep his restaurants open, adding to a growing backlash over the Chancellor’s raid on hospitality.

The Michelin-starred chef said Rachel Reeves’s business rates overhaul would more than double his property tax bills, with the Chancellor offering “no additional support or benefit”.

Mr Kerridge said the increase threatened to make it more difficult to run his business, adding: “It tightens the pressure on cash flow and limits our ability to create jobs, invest in improvements or even keep our doors open.”

The TV chef runs a number of acclaimed pubs and restaurants in Marlow, Buckinghamshire; and in London.

Mr Kerridge signed a letter in support for Labour ahead of the election last year and said that while he still backed the party, he disagreed with Ms Reeves’s business rates changes.

He told The Telegraph: “If hospitality venues across the country face the same burden, it will directly impact local communities and the vibrancy of our towns and cities.”

It comes amid mounting criticism over the Chancellor’s reforms to property taxes, which she claimed would level the playing field between online giants and the high street.

Ms Reeves previously suggested long-awaited reforms of the business rates system would push up tax bills for large warehouses used by online sellers to fund cuts for independent businesses.

In the Budget, she said her reforms would reduce tax rates for pubs, restaurants and small shops to their lowest level in more than three decades – unveiling a permanent 5pc cut for hospitality and retail businesses.

However, hospitality and retail bosses have blasted the overhaul, claiming the Chancellor in fact launched a stealth raid on them at the Budget. This is because the tax cut came alongside the end of Covid-era reliefs.

Changes in the way properties are valued means bills rising, even after the 5pc headline reduction is factored in.

‘Not fair’

The average pub will face a £1,400 rise in its property tax bill next year, according to UKHospitality. The British Independent Retailers Association (Bira) estimates that smaller shops will see their business rates bill increase by 15pc.

On Wednesday, Andrew Goodacre, the boss of Bira, said the shake-up was “not fair”, adding: “This does not support the high streets. This is not levelling the playing field.”

Some pub and restaurant owners have barred Labour MPs from their venues in protest against the shock tax rises.

Meanwhile, more than a dozen trade bodies representing concert halls, orchestras and arenas have penned an open letter to Sir Keir Starmer, urging the Prime Minister to intervene before hundreds of venues are forced to shut.

Sir Keir has been warned that the increased property tax burden on arenas and concert halls will push up ticket prices, “undermining the Government’s own efforts to combat the cost of living crisis”.

Many arenas are bracing for their business rates bill to double from next year. Smaller venues “will teeter on the edge of closure, potentially resulting in thousands of job losses and hollowing out the cultural spaces that keep places thriving,” the letter said.

Mark Davyd, of the Music Venue Trust, said: “The Government needs to decide what sort of businesses will be able to operate in our high streets and villages.

“We already have buildings that are too expensive to host live music – they will have to be repurposed. I think we will end up with a lot of empty buildings.”

data.title: Crackdown on sexual content in Japanese cartoons
data.description:
Japanese-style cartoons pose a risk to children because of their “sexualised” content, the film watchdog has warned.

Most parents assume “anime” cartoon films and videos are “inherently child-friendly” when, in fact, as many as a third are being rated as 15 or 18, which is only suitable for adults, David Austin, the chief executive of the British Board of Film Classification (BBFC), said.

Writing for The Telegraph, he said research he had commissioned showed nearly nine in 10 people felt there was a potential child protection risk if anime content was not properly age-rated.

He issued the warning amid a surge in the popularity of anime, a genre of animation originating from Japan which often includes lingering or close-up shots of young characters’ bodies and gratuitous nudity.

Adult content

Mr Austin said the BBFC had taken a “responsible” approach to protect children where a third of the films or videos had been classified as 15 or 18 because of their “adult” or sexualised content.

“In an era where just a few swipes can expose children to almost anything online, age ratings are a practical, empowering tool; providing trusted guidance for parents and carers seeking reassurance that what their children watch is appropriate,” said Mr Austin.

“As anime continues its rapid rise in British popular culture, there is a growing responsibility to protect children from unsuitable content.”

Nearly nine in 10 (88 per cent) of people agreed there was a child protection risk if anime content was not age-rated appropriately and consistently because parents not familiar with the genre assumed it was child friendly, according to a survey of more than 2,100 people aged over 16.

More than two thirds (69 per cent) said sexualised nudity in anime should be rated higher than natural nudity. Participants in the research, which included focus groups, highlighted certain visual techniques which they said could shift nudity from natural to sexualised.

These included lingering shots, close-ups or camera angles emphasising characters’ physical attributes which added a sexualised dimension, regardless of the narrative context. Eight in 10 identified these as “aggravating” factors that justified higher age classifications.

‘Lingering shots’

“There were quite a few lingering shots on her breasts and buttocks, which wouldn’t have been appropriate for a younger audience,” one parent unfamiliar with the genre told the researchers.

Critics have argued that Japanese anime is rife with sexist themes, among which are sexualised underage female characters, career-obsessed female villains and sexual assault scenes portrayed as acceptable expressions of love or admiration.

They cite the popular anime Doraemon, where the male protagonist’s constant desire to sneak a peek at his crush – who is a primary school student – in the shower has, they say, been rationalised as a harmless gesture of romantic admiration.

According to the survey for BBFC, the misuse of authority by characters against the more vulnerable was cited by 86 per cent of respondents as one of the most significant factors to justify higher ratings of 12, 15 or 18.

Some 82 per cent cited characters who appear to be children or behave in a childlike way was another reason for higher content classifications.

Eight in 10 said the same about so-called “fan service”, sexual content including gratuitous nudity or shots to titillate the viewer rather than being essential to the plot or character development.

Audiences identified several factors that helped mitigate concerns around sexualised content, including the absence of nudity or explicit sexual activity, the use of humour, and instances where problematic behaviour was challenged or condemned.

However, tolerance decreased when sexualisation was perceived as gratuitous, such as through overt “fan service.”

Sexual content in anime has as much impact as in live-action

By David Austin

Anime is no longer a niche fascination: it’s a British box-office force. With this year’s Demon Slayer: Kimetsu No Yaiba: Infinity Castle delivering the biggest ever opening for an anime release at the UK box office, it’s clear that anime’s vibrant storytelling and striking visuals have captured the nation’s imagination.

Beyond the cinema, anime’s popularity is evident in its resurgence on physical media. In 2025 so far, anime has accounted for nearly a quarter of content classified by the BBFC for release on DVD and Blu-ray – a share that has more than doubled in recent years.

As anime continues its rapid rise in British popular culture, there is a growing responsibility to protect children from unsuitable content. New research we’ve published today found that most parents assume anime is inherently child-friendly. But while two-thirds of anime classified so far for physical media release in 2025 received age ratings between U and 12, a significant one-third was rated 15 or 18.

Age ratings and clear content advice for anime are not just helpful – they are essential tools for parents and caregivers seeking to ensure age-appropriate viewing, according to 90 per cent of people in our new research. The study, which explored how UK audiences (including anime fans, non-fans, teenagers, and parents) assess sexualised content in anime found that 88 per cent think there is a potential child protection risk if anime content is not age-rated appropriately and consistently.

In line with the BBFC’s current approach, British audiences told us that sexual content in anime is just as impactful as in live-action and should be classified accordingly. Nine in ten agreed that techniques which emphasise a character’s physical attributes – such as lingering or close-up shots – add a sexualised dimension to nudity and were identified as aggravating factors that may require higher age ratings.

Power imbalances were seen as the strongest aggravator: 86 per cent said that when a sexual scene involves an authority figure abusing their position, the BBFC should classify the content at a higher age rating. Similarly, nearly 80 per cent agreed that gratuitous “fan service” intended to titillate the viewer should elevate a work into a higher classification. Importantly, these concerns were shared not only by non-fans but by anime fans as well.

The BBFC’s approach to classifying anime – combining age ratings with clear and detailed content advice – is strongly supported by the public. The BBFC age rating system was also identified as the most recognised and most trusted when tested against alternative systems currently used on some streaming services.

The findings also demonstrated a strong demand for consistency. Ninety-one per cent believe age ratings should be applied consistently across all platforms, including streaming services. There is no legal requirement for streaming services to carry BBFC age ratings in the UK, yet many including Netflix and Prime Video have voluntarily adopted the BBFC system. There are, however, some services using ratings that do not reflect the expectations of British audiences.

Consistency matters: when guidance is inconsistent or lacks transparency, the risk of children encountering unsuitable content increases. In an era where just a few swipes can expose children to almost anything online, age ratings are a practical, empowering tool; providing trusted guidance for parents and carers seeking reassurance that what their children watch is appropriate.

David Austin is chief executive of the British Board of Film Classification

data.title: Democrats threaten to unravel Paramount power grab post-Trump
data.description:
Democrats have threatened to unravel Paramount’s proposed takeover of Warner Bros once Donald Trump, the US president, is out of power.

Warner Bros had agreed to an $83bn (£62bn) takeover by Netflix for its studio and streaming networks but is now fending off a power grab by Paramount, which has taken its offer straight to shareholders.

Two Democratic Representatives have written to the board of Warner Bros Discovery and to Scott Bessent, the US treasury secretary, to raise concerns about the Hollywood studios being sold to a buyer backed by foreign powers.

Paramount’s $108bn hostile bid is primarily funded by the sovereign wealth funds of Saudi Arabia, Abu Dhabi and Qatar.

In the letter, seen by Semafor, Sam Liccardo and Ayanna Pressley wrote: “Future Congresses will review many of the decisions of the current administration and may recommend that regulators push for divestitures, which would undermine the strategic logic of this merger.

“We urge the board to weigh these national security and regulatory liabilities in evaluating a transaction burdened by uncertain but potentially extensive mitigation obligations, foreign influence risks or adverse regulatory action.”

Together, the Gulf states are contributing $24bn to the bid – double the amount that Paramount’s main shareholders, the Ellison family, are putting in and accounting for almost three fifths of the total equity.

Jared Kushner, Mr Trump’s son-in-law, is also backing the bid through his investment firm, Affinity Partners.

Paramount has insisted that the three wealth funds – as well as Mr Kushner – will forgo any governance rights as part of the deal, including board seats.

It has insisted that this puts its bid outside the scope of the committee on foreign investment in the United States (CFIUS), which reviews the potential threat of investments to national security.

But the two Democrats, who both sit on the US House committee on financial services, warned that any failure to seek a CFIUS investigation would represent a “serious lapse in fiduciary judgment” that could “expose the company to significant regulatory and reputational harm”.

They added: “These investors, by virtue of their financial position or contractual rights, could obtain influence – direct or indirect – over business decisions that bear upon editorial independence, content moderation, distribution priorities or the stewardship of Americans’ private data.

“Even absent overt control, such influence can present a national security threat when foreign state-linked entities have strategic interests inconsistent with those of the United States.”

The threat underscores the political sensitivities around the future of Warner Bros.

While Democrats are currently unable to intervene in the deal, an expected period of lengthy regulatory scrutiny means it may drag on towards the next presidential election in 2028.

David Ellison, the chairman and chief executive of Paramount, has reportedly met with Warner investors to boost his chances of a deal, which is for the entire company.

Paramount is now said to be considering increasing its offer.

So far, it has tabled a bid of $30 per share – compared to Netflix’s $27.75 offer – but analysts think the studio giant could increase this to as much as $35.

data.title: Gerald Ratner plots to buy back jewellery empire he ruined
data.description:
Gerald Ratner has launched an audacious bid to buy back the UK arm of the jewellery empire he ruined with his infamous gaffe more than three decades ago.

The businessman, 76, is seeking to acquire the H Samuel and Ernest Jones chains from US-listed Signet Jewelers and install himself as chairman after he lost control of the businesses in the early 90s.

Mr Ratner has launched an appeal to the shareholders behind the brands as part of a bid to purchase the loss-making UK arms, which he said he has been pursuing since the summer.

The brands were once part of Ratners Group, the firm that Mr Ratner was forced to exit after he declared their products were “total c--p” in a speech at the Institute of Directors.

Mr Ratner also remarked that some of the firm’s earrings were “cheaper than a prawn sandwich at Marks & Spencer – but I have to say, the sandwich will probably last longer than the earrings”.

The fallout gave rise to the phrase “to do a Ratner”, which means to make a disastrous own goal.

Mr Ratner said he was attempting to acquire the UK division of Signet – which was formerly Ratners Group before it was rebranded – because he claimed its American owners were “doing everything wrong”.

To launch his bid, Mr Ratner said he has been in touch with Signet’s chief executive via a bank in New York that is acting on his behalf.

He is now launching an appeal directly to the company’s shareholders, who Mr Ratner hopes will start questioning why the US owners do not sell the loss-making division.

Mr Ratner told The Telegraph: “The reason we’re putting pressure on the shareholders is simply because of the fact that they’re doing so badly in the UK, they’re closing shops all the time and last year they sold their best shops.

“So we took the view that they’re not really interested in the UK. We approached them thinking that it’s in the interests of shareholders to just get rid of it.”

Signet, which moved its primary listing from London to New York in 2008, is worth more than $3.7bn (£2.8bn).

While its US business is successful, the UK division is underperforming with losses before tax rising from £483,000 to £1.4m in the year to February, accounts show.

Mr Ratner said: “They’re getting everything wrong. When I had shops in America, I didn’t sell anything that I sold in the UK. They speak the same language, that’s about it. Everything is different. Every product is different. The way they buy is different.

“What the Americans have tried to do is make the classic mistake and try to transport a formula across the Atlantic. It has never worked and it never will work. The first rule is that you’ve got to run businesses totally differently to suit the local market.”

Mr Ratner said he would overhaul the shop’s offerings to sell more lower-priced goods, adding: “I would offer fantastic value for money.”

He is backed by a consortium of investors who are primarily British, with one American. He said they have the funds lined up.

Mr Ratner added: “I did have 2,500 shops. I had the American business, I had Ratners, I had watches – and it was a much bigger thing. So it’s nothing like the idea that I’m coming back exactly as it was.

“But I would very much like to try and get that business back on its feet. It would be a wonderful experience. I’d love it.”

Signet was contacted for comment.

data.description:
Gerald Ratner has launched an audacious bid to buy back the UK arm of the jewellery empire he ruined with his infamous gaffe more than three decades ago.

The businessman, 76, is seeking to acquire the H Samuel and Ernest Jones chains from US-listed Signet Jewelers and install himself as chairman after he lost control of the businesses in the early 90s.

Mr Ratner has launched an appeal to the shareholders behind the brands as part of a bid to purchase the loss-making UK arms, which he said he has been pursuing since the summer.

The brands were once part of Ratners Group, the firm that Mr Ratner was forced to exit after he declared their products were “total c--p” in a speech at the Institute of Directors.

Mr Ratner also remarked that some of the firm’s earrings were “cheaper than a prawn sandwich at Marks & Spencer – but I have to say, the sandwich will probably last longer than the earrings”.

The fallout gave rise to the phrase “to do a Ratner”, which means to make a disastrous own goal.

Mr Ratner said he was attempting to acquire the UK division of Signet – which was formerly Ratners Group before it was rebranded – because he claimed its American owners were “doing everything wrong”.

To launch his bid, Mr Ratner said he has been in touch with Signet’s chief executive via a bank in New York that is acting on his behalf.

He is now launching an appeal directly to the company’s shareholders, who Mr Ratner hopes will start questioning why the US owners do not sell the loss-making division.

Mr Ratner told The Telegraph: “The reason we’re putting pressure on the shareholders is simply because of the fact that they’re doing so badly in the UK, they’re closing shops all the time and last year they sold their best shops.

“So we took the view that they’re not really interested in the UK. We approached them thinking that it’s in the interests of shareholders to just get rid of it.”

Signet, which moved its primary listing from London to New York in 2008, is worth more than $3.7bn (£2.8bn).

While its US business is successful, the UK division is underperforming with losses before tax rising from £483,000 to £1.4m in the year to February, accounts show.

Mr Ratner said: “They’re getting everything wrong. When I had shops in America, I didn’t sell anything that I sold in the UK. They speak the same language, that’s about it. Everything is different. Every product is different. The way they buy is different.

“What the Americans have tried to do is make the classic mistake and try to transport a formula across the Atlantic. It has never worked and it never will work. The first rule is that you’ve got to run businesses totally differently to suit the local market.”

Mr Ratner said he would overhaul the shop’s offerings to sell more lower-priced goods, adding: “I would offer fantastic value for money.”

He is backed by a consortium of investors who are primarily British, with one American. He said they have the funds lined up.

Mr Ratner added: “I did have 2,500 shops. I had the American business, I had Ratners, I had watches – and it was a much bigger thing. So it’s nothing like the idea that I’m coming back exactly as it was.

“But I would very much like to try and get that business back on its feet. It would be a wonderful experience. I’d love it.”

Signet was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Moment gas explosion destroys home near San Francisco
data.description:
Two people are missing after a gas explosion in the San Francisco Bay Area.

The blast in Hayward, which left six people in hospital and razed at least one home, triggered a major fire, officials said.

Video footage showed a home suddenly exploding, throwing debris into the air.

“We were sitting in the house and it just... everything shook,” said Brittany Maldonado, who lives across the street.

“Stuff fell off the walls and when we looked at the camera, it was like you were watching a war video.”

Fire officials said they did not know if those injured were workers or residents, or the extent of their injuries.

They said two homes were affected the most, along with another building.

The neighbourhood had been recently undergoing construction work for wider paths and bike lanes.

Hayward, home to about 160,000 residents, is in the East Bay, 15 miles (24km) south of Oakland.

A spokesman with Pacific Gas & Electric Co (PG&E) said a construction crew damaged an underground gas line at around 7.35am.

The company said it was not their workers who damaged the line.

Utility workers isolated the damaged line and stopped the flow of gas at 9.25am, PG&E said.

The doorbell video showed a large excavator being used to dig in front of the home that exploded as a worker stood nearby.

After the explosion, people ran towards the home to search for victims.

Several workers lifted a large piece of debris from where it landed near the excavator.

Sirens could be heard in the distance as police arrived at the scene, while flames began to spread at the site of the demolished building.

data.title: Thief destroys bank wall to extract cash machine
data.description:
A thief used a forklift to prise a cash machine from a Nationwide shopfront, completely destroying a wall in the process.

Pictures from the scene in the early hours of Friday in Towcester, Northamptonshire, show the smashed entrance, with bricks and broken glass strewn across the ground.

Northamptonshire Police said a telehandler, also known as a reach forklift, was stolen from a nearby garden centre before the thief extracted the ATM from the wall.

Several locals initially claimed the machine had been empty, but Nationwide confirmed to The Telegraph it had contained money.

Police urged any witnesses to the ram raid to come forward, but said no arrests had been made.

Gallone’s ice cream parlour, which is located opposite the bank, said Nationwide staff were “unharmed but shaken and in disbelief”.

“As you can see, we’ve had the most callous of incidents during the night,” they said.

“As a tight-knit community, we are resolute that this will not deter us in any way, shape or form.”

A force spokesman said: “Detectives are appealing for witnesses following a ram raid incident in Towcester.

“The incident happened in the early hours of this morning (Dec 5) when a telehandler was stolen from Payne’s Nurseries in Watling Street.

“It was then used to steal the ATM at the Nationwide Building Society branch in Sponne Arcade.”

A Nationwide spokesman said: “We are aware of the incident at our branch in Towcester which happened early this morning.

“We are working to re-open the branch and get the ATM replaced as soon as possible.

“In the meantime, customers can visit one of our nearby branches for face-to-face service, such as in Stony Stratford or Northampton.

“We recommend checking locations and opening hours on our website.”

The bank said it was unable to comment on how much money had been taken in the incident.

data.title: Chinese car sales double in Europe despite tariffs
data.description:
Chinese car sales have almost doubled in Europe despite swingeing trade tariffs, delivering a blow to the region’s domestic manufacturers.

The number of cars sold by Chinese companies increased to 509,700 in the first nine months of 2025 – up by 91pc compared with the 266,600 sold in 2024, according to Car Industry Analysis.

This included big jumps in sales by companies such as BYD, Leapmotor, Dongfeng and Chery, which owns the Jaecoo and Omoda brands.

The surge is likely to trigger concern in Brussels after the European Union slapped tariffs of up to 45pc on Chinese electric cars that are shipped to the bloc. This followed an investigation that found they were being unfairly subsidised by Beijing.

Chinese brands have faced an additional tariff of up to 35pc on imports, in addition to the existing tariff of 10pc on foreign cars, since November 2024.

But the sales figures – which cover the EU along with the UK, Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland – suggest they have compensated by shifting their focus away from fully electric to hybrids and plug-in hybrids, which accounted for most of the increase, according to Car Industry Analysis.

Saic Motor, which owns MG, sold the most, accounting for 226,047 cars, or an increase of 21pc on the previous year. The sales of Leapmotor, which has a partnership with Vauxhall owner Stellantis, surged by 8,600pc.

Skyworth, BAIC, Dongfeng and BYD notched up increases of 582pc, 532pc, 430pc and 305pc respectively.

Felipe Muñoz, an independent expert who runs Car Industry Analysis, said the “very strong” growth, first reported by Automotive News Europe, suggested Chinese firms had decided to “offset the losses from lower volumes of electric cars by focusing on plug-in hybrids and hybrids.”

He added: “That’s why they’re still growing. And in some cases they are still growing with electric cars, because their prices are not as high as the Western cars and, in general, their products are really good.

“We should expect to see growth next year as well, because they are introducing even more models.

“Some of them are considering new technologies, such as the extended-range vehicles. And some are due to start producing locally, so that would also help them.”

Chinese carmakers have been flooding Europe with their vehicles amid brutal price wars at home, particularly in the electric vehicle (EV) market.

It has triggered fears that Europe’s car industry faces an existential threat from a wave of low-cost Chinese models.

The EU announced its tariffs on Chinese EVs last year following an eight-month investigation that claimed they “benefit from unfair subsidies”.

A report argued “the entire BEV [battery electric vehicle] value chain is heavily subsidised in China, and imports of Chinese BEVs presented a threat of clearly foreseeable and imminent injury to EU industry”.

Talks have been ongoing between Brussels and Beijing to settle the row but so far no deal has been reached.

The Chinese government has branded the tariffs “a typical case of protectionism”.

The European Commission was approached for comment.

data.title: Renationalised trains to be red, white and blue
data.description:
Renationalised trains will be given a Union flag-inspired red, white and blue livery, the Government has announced.

State-owned company Great British Railways (GBR) is to unveil its branding on Tuesday, before it is rolled out to trains, websites and stations from next spring.

Stephen Bayley, founder of the Design Museum, criticised the new colour scheme.

Mr Bayley said: “It’s atrocious. A mad dog’s breakfast. A livery is branding and branding is all about associations and expectations.

“In this sense, they’ve got it right. It projects the values of the sponsoring organisation: artless, careless, clumsy, unintelligent and uncoordinated.”

Referring to Japan’s bullet trains, he continued: “Have they never seen a Japanese Shinkansen? That’s what a train should look like.

“We have some of the best designers in the world in this country. A pity they were not hired by Great British Railways.”

However, the Department for Transport said the “striking and memorable design” was developed in-house, to maximise value for money.

The new livery will mark a stark change from the “Rail Blue” colour scheme that defined publicly owned British Rail’s trains from the mid-1960s to the 1980s.

Christian Wolmar, a rail historian and author, said he found the new GBR livery “impressive”, saying: “I think it conveys an impression of speed. It’s elegant. It combines Union Jack colours, and I think it’s very important to have an identity.

“The design is bloody fabulous. I just wonder if it’s a bit too modern, whether they could have reflected at all on the past, but I have to commend it.”

The seven major train operators that have already nationalised have kept the branding of their previous operators.

The Government has said that existing train companies will rebrand as Great British Railways, a process that has now been brought forward to next year from the expected date of 2027.

Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, said: “I’m immensely proud to unveil the new look for Great British Railways as we deliver landmark legislation to nationalise our trains and reform the railway so it better serves passengers.

“This isn’t just a paint job, it represents a new railway, casting off the frustrations of the past and focused entirely on delivering a proper public service for passengers.

“With fares frozen, a bold new look and fundamental reforms becoming law, we are building a railway Britain can rely on and be proud of.”

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Channel 4 poaches Sky exec to head up broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4 has poached an advertising executive from Sky to head up the youth-focused broadcaster as it grapples with an uncertain future.

Priya Dogra, 46, has been selected by Channel 4’s board as the eighth chief executive in the broadcaster’s 43-year history.

The Canadian served as a top executive at Warner Bros Discovery (WBD) for 14 years, overseeing the Hollywood giant’s operations in Europe, the Middle East and Africa.

She also led local production for HBO, the network behind TV hits including Game of Thrones and Succession.

Most recently she was Sky’s chief advertising and new revenue officer, responsible for overseeing Sky Media which handles ads for the media giant’s own channels as well as for other broadcasters such as WBD and Paramount.

She joined after Sky became embroiled in a major accounting blunder that saw it underpay ad partners by hundreds of millions of pounds over several years.

Ms Dogra’s departure will leave Sky without its most senior advertising executive just as it is locked in discussions over a takeover of ITV’s television business in a deal that will combine the UK’s two largest ad sales houses.

Ms Dogra, who will be Channel 4’s second female chief executive following predecessor Alex Mahon, will take up the role in March.

The TV executive has emerged victorious following a closely guarded recruitment process led by executive search firm Spencer Stuart.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who took the top job on an interim basis, is believed to have made it to the final stages, while top Netflix executive Emma Lloyd is also thought to have made the shortlist.

A number of other high-profile candidates, including Jane Turton, chief executive of Traitors producer All3Media, ruled themselves out of the process.

Ms Dogra has been responsible for overseeing Sky Media, the advertising sales arm that handles ads for the media giant’s own channels as well as for other broadcasters such as WBD and Paramount.

She joined after Sky became embroiled in a major accounting blunder that saw it underpay ad partners by hundreds of millions of pounds over several years.

The appointment marks the first major decision by Geoff Cooper, the former AO World boss who was named Channel 4 chairman in September.

It ends a protracted period of uncertainty for the public service broadcaster after former chief executive Ms Mahon left over the summer. Sir Ian Cheshire, the company’s previous chairman, stood down in April.

Nevertheless, it comes at a critical time for Channel 4 as it navigates a troubled shift to the digital era.

Ms Dogra will be tasked with accelerating the channel’s streaming push and securing commercial agreements to help offset the decline in ad revenues from its traditional linear business.

The broadcaster has been given the green light to begin in-house production after rules banning it from making its own shows were relaxed following an unsuccessful attempt to privatise the broadcaster by the previous government.

British TV bosses have also called for an easing of regulations to allow for greater collaboration between traditional broadcasters as they seek to fight back against deep-pocketed streaming rivals such as Netflix and Disney.

Channel 4 recently inked a deal with BBC-owned UKTV to show classic BBC shows such as The Office, Red Dwarf and QI on its streaming service.

Ms Dogra also spearheaded a recent partnership between Channel 4, ITV and Sky that will see the broadcasters pool their advertising space in a bid to win over more brands.

However, Channel 4 is contending with a major redrawing of the media landscape as ITV is locked in talks to sell its television business to Sky’s US owner Comcast.

In the US, Warner Bros is also locked in a fierce takeover battle as Larry Ellison’s Paramount seeks to gatecrash an $83bn (£62bn) deal between the Hollywood studio giant and Netflix.

Channel 4, ITV and Sky have also forged a partnership that will see the broadcasters pool their advertising space in a bid to win over more brands.

More drastic change is also under way as ITV is locked in talks to sell its television business to Sky’s US owner Comcast.

Channel 4 has previously been linked to a merger with the BBC, though both broadcasters have denied it. However, the future of the BBC is under particular scrutiny as it enters negotiations to renew its Royal Charter amid a crisis over alleged bias.

Mr Cooper, Channel 4 chairman, said: “Priya is an outstanding executive, a visionary leader and has a formidable intellect.

“She also possesses a passion for Channel 4 and a deep understanding of its public service remit. Priya is ideally equipped to lead the business through its next chapter, and we are thrilled to welcome her aboard.”

Ms Dogra said: “Joining Channel 4 at this moment is a genuine privilege. Few organisations sit so firmly at the heart of British culture or have such a clear purpose and vital mission: to challenge, to reflect and represent voices across the UK, and to spark change through entertainment.”

data.title: Disney gives Star Wars and Marvel rights to AI giant in $1bn deal
data.description:
Disney has struck a landmark $1bn (£750m) deal with the company behind ChatGPT to sell the rights to characters from Star Wars and Marvel.

The Hollywood film giant announced a three-year tie-up with OpenAI on Thursday, granting the tech giant access to more than 200 Disney characters.

It will allow fans to create images and short films featuring Disney personalities through OpenAI’s technology, including on ChatGPT and the Sora video generation app.

Disney will invest $1bn in OpenAI as part of the agreement – which covers characters and franchises such as Mickey Mouse, Lilo and Stitch, Frozen, Moana, Toy Story and Zootopia as well as Marvel and Lucasfilm characters.

Despite handing over some of the character rights, the deal will not cover voices or actors’ likenesses, while the $500bn start-up will also be obliged to ensure they are depicted in an age-appropriate manner.

Shares in Disney rose by 1.5pc following the deal.

Bob Iger, Disney’s chief executive, hailed the agreement as a means to combine AI with the company’s “iconic stories and characters”, describing it as a way to “responsibly extend the reach of our storytelling”.

He also said it would place “imagination and creativity directly into the hands of Disney fans in ways we’ve never seen before”.

Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive, added: “This agreement shows how AI companies and creative leaders can work together responsibly to promote innovation that benefits society, respect the importance of creativity and help works reach vast new audiences.”

The deal has been struck despite growing tensions between the AI industry and Hollywood, as some of the biggest film studios have sued technology companies for alleged infringement of intellectual property rights.

Disney, for instance, has sued AI firm Midjourney over its image generator, which it has described as a “bottomless pit of plagiarism”.

Midjourney has rejected the claims and said studios are seeking to “stifle” creativity and innovation.

On Wednesday, Disney reportedly wrote a cease and desist letter to Google, the internet search giant and rival to OpenAI, warning that it was “infringing Disney’s copyrights on a massive scale” with its own AI tools. Google was contacted for comment.

Tensions have also escalated after some AI companies promoted entirely synthetic actors and influencers, such as one AI actress known as Tilly Norwood.

Mark Hamill, the actor who played Luke Skywalker in the original Star Wars trilogy, has labelled AI actors “terrible, ghastly, ghoulish, weird”.

Movie fans have widely derided AI creations as “slop”.

Fears over AI have also spread to other areas of entertainment.

Artists and musicians have accused AI companies of posing a threat to their livelihood. This is because apps can generate lifelike characters and voice-overs.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Delaying Ed Miliband’s net zero targets ‘could save UK £350bn’
data.description:
Slowing the pace of net zero could save the British economy £350bn, an official report has concluded.

A new analysis published by the National Energy System Operator (Neso) said that aiming for an 80pc reduction in carbon emissions by 2050, rather than the current “net zero” target, could save £14bn a year on average.

The Conservatives claimed the estimates showed that “rushing to net zero” would lumber the UK with sky-high electricity prices for decades and leave Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, unable to meet his promise of cutting household bills by £300.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “For years, the public has been told the lie that net zero means cheaper energy.

“This report shows the truth – rushing to net zero is forecast to make our energy system £350bn more expensive than going slower.

“Britain cannot afford to spend the next 25 years having uncompetitive electricity prices. We would continue to deindustrialise, miss out on the economic growth of AI and see living standards suffer.”

Mr Miliband’s department dismissed the findings as merely “illustrative” and said it was possible that the costs of renewables could fall faster than was assumed. It also pointed to large variations in some of the estimates.

The Neso report, published on Thursday, explores the potential economic impact of different scenarios, including a “holistic transition” in which the UK achieves net zero carbon emissions by 2050, and another, called “falling behind”, in which emissions only fall by 78pc compared with 1990 levels.

Analysis found that the “falling behind” scenario, in which emissions were cut at a slower pace, was £14bn per year cheaper than the net zero transition.

Most of the savings come from households and businesses spending less on new heating systems and power plants, respectively.

However, Neso cautioned that its estimates were highly sensitive to changes in its assumptions. For example, if gas prices rise above current levels, the savings from going slower drop to £5bn a year, or £125bn over 2025-50.

If they fall further, annual savings could rise to £19bn – or £475bn over a 25-year period.

The report added that reaching net zero at a slower pace would only spread the costs over a long period, rather than eliminating them completely.

This would also leave Britain exposed to “volatile” gas prices for longer and delay other benefits of green energy, such as domestic jobs, improved air quality and better health outcomes from warmer homes.

Neso said achieving net zero was only cheaper overall when the “carbon value” – a government-calculated figure that seeks to measure prevented climate harms – was factored in.

The quango’s report is likely to prompt fresh debate about the pace of the UK’s transition to net zero carbon emissions at a time when critics have accused the Government of pushing up costs by committing to ambitious timescales.

Mr Miliband wants 95pc of electricity to be generated by “clean” sources such as wind and solar from 2030. This will require a near-tripling of offshore wind capacity and a similar jump in solar capacity.

The Energy Secretary has claimed this investment blitz will cut annual household bills by £300 a year and that Britain has a responsibility to lead the world through “the power of example”.

But his critics argue his plans will pile costs on to consumers, partly through the rising cost of renewable energy subsidies and network charges that are added to bills.

Ms Coutinho warned that “overbuilding” would lead to higher “constraint” payments when wind farms are paid to switch off because the grid is too congested to accept their power.

She added: “Ed Miliband’s Clean Power 2030 is not in the public’s interest – it’s only multi-million-pound wind developers who will profit.”

At her party’s annual conference in October, Ms Coutinho pledged to abolish the UK’s legally binding climate targets and enact a “cheap power plan” if the Tories were elected again.

It marks a policy reversal after Theresa May’s Conservative government enshrined the UK’s net zero commitments in law in 2019.

Responding to the Neso report, a spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “We fundamentally reject the idea that these illustrative scenarios accurately reflect the cost of moving to clean energy, which has enormous benefits in bringing down bills for good, energy security and securing well-paid, skilled jobs.

“As Neso points out, they do not reflect or predict the cost of net zero, recognising the likely path is highly dependent on future fuel prices and the advancing pace of private sector rollout of net zero technologies.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Tax on rental income: What is it, calculations and how to pay
data.description:
In the same way that workers must pay tax on their earnings, landlords also have to pay tax on the income they receive from tenants.

From April 2027, this will get even more expensive, as the Government unveiled plans in the 2025 Budget to increase rental income tax by two percentage points.

While buy-to-let investors used to pay tax on rental income “net of mortgage costs”, they can now only claim a 20pc tax credit on their mortgage interest payments, which has eaten into profits.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how tax on rental income is calculated, and what it means if you have multiple properties and rentals overseas.

  • What is tax on rental income?
  • How is tax on rental income calculated?
  • Rental income tax on multiple properties
  • Rental income tax on properties overseas
  • Rental income tax FAQs

What is tax on rental income?

Tax on rental income refers to income tax, which is payable on the money you receive from tenants. The rates, thresholds and tax-free allowances are currently the same as for any other type of income, whether it’s from employment, or a pension income. 

However, from April 2027 rental income will incur higher rates of tax. Basic-rate taxpayers will pay 22pc on rental income, while those who pay higher-rate tax will pay 42pc and it’s 47pc for additional-rate payers.

You can also use certain costs incurred as part of being a landlord to reduce your tax bill – but since the rules changed a few years ago, you can now offset less of your mortgage costs.

Chris Etherington, of accountants RSM UK, said: “For residential lettings, there are special provisions for mortgage and other loan finance costs. Relief for these is given as a reduction in tax, rather than set against rental profits, at a rate of 20pc.”

How is tax on rental income calculated?

Tax on rental income works differently depending on whether you are taxed as an individual, or as a company, if you have incorporated the ownership of the property.

If you are taxed as an individual, the income tax will work in much the same way as the tax you would pay on income earned from employment.

Rachael Griffin, of wealth manager Quilter, said: “It is taxed at 20pc, 40pc or 45pc, depending on your tax bracket. The main difference is that you would need to complete a self-assessment tax return and calculate your profits.”

The amount you need to pay is determined by subtracting any tax-free allowances and expenses from your total rental income – resulting in your taxable profit.

As mentioned above, this profit is then taxed according to your income band.

For the 2025-26 tax year:

  • Basic-rate taxpayers pay 20pc on income between £12,570-£50,270
  • Higher-rate taxpayers pay 40pc on income between £50,271-£125,140
  • Additional-rate taxpayers pay 45pc on income over £125,140.

While most taxpayers pay nothing on earnings up to £12,570 – the personal allowance – this reduces once you earn more than £100,000; for every £2 extra you earn, you lose £1 of the tax-free allowance. By the time you earn £125,140, you’ll have no personal allowance at all.

As the UK has a progressive tax system, be aware that you need to add any rent from letting a property with any other taxable income to determine the tax rate that may apply.

Ms Griffin added: “For example, if you were earning £35,000 from employment and earned a further £20,000 from rental income, you would be pushed into the higher rate of income tax and would therefore pay the higher rate of 40pc on £4,730.”

Why do you pay tax on rental income?

  • Rental income is considered taxable earnings, like a salary or business profit
  • Paying tax on rental income is a legal obligation
  • While other investments can benefit from being held in an Isa (individual savings account) where growth is tax-free, the Government does not incentivise investing in property in the same way
  • Even though the property tax rules have been put together by different governments over time, a key reason for the current tax rules could be due to the lack of housing supply. The rules in place at present could potentially deter many would-be landlords from starting – or continuing – rental businesses, in theory increasing the number of properties available for owner-occupiers.

Rental income tax on multiple properties

If you have multiple properties, you will typically need to pool together all of the profits and treat them as income from one business. You then deduct allowable expenses for each property to calculate your total taxable profit.

Nicholas Mendes, of broker John Charcol, said: “For example, if one generates a profit and another operates at a loss, the loss from one property can offset the profit from another – potentially reducing your overall tax liability.”

To make this process easier, it is highly advisable to keep separate, detailed records for each property, covering both income and expenses.

Mr Mendes added: “This ensures accuracy and simplifies the process of filing your tax return.”

Note though, that there are some exceptions to the guidelines set out above.

Mr Etherington said: “This includes rental income from properties held in partnership which would need to be declared as a separate rental business.”

Rental income tax on properties overseas

Be aware that profits from an overseas rental property can still be taxable in the UK, even if tax has been paid overseas as well.

Mr Mendes said: “Owning a property overseas adds an extra layer of complexity to your tax obligation. Fortunately, the UK has double taxation agreements with many countries, allowing you to claim relief and avoid being taxed twice on the same income.”

But as navigating the specific tax rules in both jurisdictions can be challenging, it’s worth seeking professional advice to ensure compliance – and also to make sure you don’t end up overpaying on tax.

When to pay rental income tax

The following are the deadlines for the 2024-25 return:

  • 31 October 2025: paper Self Assessment return deadline
  • 30 December 2025: deadline to submit online if you want HMRC to collect tax through PAYE code (for under £3,000 owed)
  • 31 January 2026: deadline for online Self Assessment return and balancing payment for 2024–25, plus first payment on account towards 2025–26
  • 31 July 2026: second payment on account for 2025–26

Failure to declare rental income can result in fines, penalties and in some cases legal action. By staying compliant, you can ensure your tax affairs are in order.

If you let a property and earn income from it, take care to stay within the rules. You will need to calculate profits and file a self-assessment tax return.

Expenses and tax allowances

Expenses:

Allowable expenses are costs which are incurred “wholly and exclusively” for the purposes of renting out a property. They can be deducted from your rental income to reduce your taxable profit. These include:

  • Utility bills
  • Property repairs
  • Service charge costs
  • Letting agent fees
  • Landlord insurance.

Ms Griffin said: “These expenses can help to reduce your taxable income when completing your self-assessment tax return, so it is important to keep a detailed log of all expenses.”

Allowances:

There are a host of property-related tax-free allowances for individual landlords. These include:

  • the property allowance, which can exempt up to £1,000 of property income a year from income tax
  • the rent-a-room scheme, which can allow an owner-occupier to receive up to £7,500 of rental income a year tax-free – provided it is received from a tenant living in their home.

Tax on rental income FAQs

Yes, you still pay tax on rental income, even if you’re paying off a mortgage.

Having a mortgage on your rental property can significantly affect how much tax you pay.

Previously, landlords could deduct the full amount of mortgage interest from their rental income, but in recent years, tax relief for landlords has been reduced.

Mr Mendes said: “Now, if you let a property, you can only claim a 20pc tax credit on your mortgage interest payments.”

This can mean that landlords who are higher-rate or additional-rate taxpayers will not receive full tax relief on their rental income.

Mr Etherington warned: “In some instances, landlords can make a loss on their rental business but still end up with a tax liability.”

This depends on your other income. If, as a landlord, you don’t have any other earnings – other than rental income – you might have no income tax to pay. This is because the rent you get may fall within your personal allowance. This allows you to earn a certain amount of income tax-free.

Most individuals get a personal allowance of £12,570 (frozen at this level until April 6 2028) – and will not usually have a tax liability if their taxable income is below this.

Additionally, landlords may benefit from the £1,000 property allowance. This can be used as an alternative to claiming actual expenses if it is more beneficial.

Yes. Rental income is taxable, even if it is your only source of income.

That said, as mentioned above, if your total income – including rental income – is below the personal allowance (of £12,570 for the 2025-26 tax year), you won’t owe any tax.

Mr Mendes said: “Despite this, you are still required to declare the income to HMRC to ensure your records are accurate and up to date. Equally, if your rental income does exceed the personal allowance, then you will be required to pay tax on the amount above the threshold.”

data.title: Madeleine McCann’s father calls for new Leveson Inquiry
data.description:
Madeleine McCann’s father is leading calls for the Leveson Inquiry into media ethics to resume.

Gerry McCann claimed lives were being “destroyed on a daily basis” by the press and said his family had been subject to “monstering” by the media.

Mr McCann and his wife Kate McCann are among signatories to a letter asking Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, to reverse a decision not to hold the second phase of the Leveson Inquiry, which investigated media practices and ethics from 2011 to 2012.

On Wednesday, the Duke of Sussex came out in support of Mr McCann, saying he “stands firmly with all those harmed by unethical and unlawful press intrusion”.

Madeleine went missing from her family’s apartment in the Portuguese resort of Praia da Luz in May 2007, when she was three years old, as her parents dined at a nearby tapas bar.

The subsequent search attracted widespread global media attention and the case remains one of the best-known of a missing person.

In a rare public interview, Mr McCann has said the media “repeatedly interfered with the investigation” into his daughter’s disappearance and he believed this hindered the search for her.

He told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme it was “not acceptable” that Labour had gone back on its commitment to start Leveson part two.

“There was a commitment by the Labour Party, if they went into power, that they would proceed with that,” he said.

“People’s lives are being damaged, destroyed on a daily basis, and it’s simply not acceptable.”

Describing the “distressing” and “disturbing” impact that media coverage had on him and his family, he said: “You can see how people succumb to and think they can’t get out of it.

“It’s so damaging, and we need that protection. There has to be redress. And at the minute, for most of these mainstream media, there is no redress.

“They don’t investigate, they don’t uphold complaints, and more importantly, the people who oversaw the culture of phone hacking and blagging, they’re still in power.”

The original inquiry was chaired by Sir Brian Leveson, a former High Court judge, who published a report in 2012 calling for oversight of the press to be overhauled following the phone hacking scandal at the News of the World.

As a result, the Press Complaints Commission, the previous regulator, was replaced by the Independent Press Standards Organisation (Ipso), which was handed greater powers.

There was going to be a second part of the inquiry, looking into the relationship between journalists and the police, but that was dropped by the Tory government in 2018.

The most controversial outcome of the first phase of the inquiry was a requirement, which was never enacted, for newspapers to sign up to a state-backed press regulator.

Mr McCann claimed media “monstering” had taken a “huge toll”. He accused the press of recently “egging-on” Julia Wandelt, the woman falsely claiming to be his missing daughter who was recently convicted of harassment.

“You don’t want these things happening. We’re fortunate, we survived, and I do mean that – we survived,” he continued.

“But I can promise you, there were times where I felt like I was drowning, and it was the media primarily, not the situation we were in.”

He added that he thought the second part of the inquiry had not happened because politicians were fearful of the press.

The McCanns are among more than 30 people to have signed a letter to the Prime Minister calling on him to reverse his decision.

Other signatories include the family of Hillsborough victims and the mother of Caroline Flack, the Love Island presenter who took her own life in 2021.

Later on Wednesday, a spokesman for Prince Harry said: “The Duke stands firmly with all those harmed by unethical and unlawful press intrusion.

“He fully supports the implementation of the Leveson Inquiry’s recommendations as essential reforms to protect the public, while safeguarding responsible, free, and fair journalism.”

The Duke has been involved in his own battle with British tabloids and in January settled legal action against News Group Newspapers, which offered a “full and unequivocal apology” for “serious intrusion” by The Sun and for phone-hacking by private investigators working for the News Of The World.

An Ipso spokesman said: “We encourage anyone with concerns about press behaviour to contact us for help.”
