data.title: Wind is our last bastion against China’s clean power takeover
data.description:
China demolished the European solar industry in the early 2000s by means of technology piracy and predatory state policies.

It has strangled Europe’s lithium battery industry in infancy, first by restricting sales of high-grade graphite, and then by darker arts.

Former employees at the bankrupt Swedish start-up Northvolt suspect that Beijing weaponised a Chinese machine contractor to sabotage the company from the inside.

China has driven European wind companies out of its domestic market, but only after extracting their secrets via forced technology transfers, then imposing an illegal 70pc local content rule, then holding back grid connections, while at the same time lavishing subsidies on Chinese rivals.

But one last bastion has yet to fall. China has not made any serious inroads into Europe’s domestic wind industry.

The Germans are determined to keep it that way. They effectively vetoed a plan to let Mingyang Smart Energy – one of China’s big four mastodons – into Luxcara’s Waterkant wind farm in the North Sea.

Luxcara says there was “no pressure” to drop Mingyang’s colossal 18.5 megawatt (MW) turbines from the project but the retreat follows a long-running political storm.

A leaked report by the German Institute for Defence and Strategic Studies (GIDS) warned that Mingyang was a potential threat to national security.

“The location lies within visual and radar range of key maritime access routes and overlaps militarily relevant training areas (“North Sea Firing Range” and “Practice Area EHD 42” off the Dutch coast),” it said.

The report said Mingyang’s private status is “politically meaningless” since all entities in China are state agents under the doctrine of civil-military fusion.

The National Security Law, Cybersecurity Law and National Intelligence Law together oblige companies to hand over data and obey orders from the Communist regime. Party commissars are lodged inside firms to enforce compliance.

Zhang Chuanwei, Mingyang’s founder, served in the Chinese army for 10 years and was a delegate to the People’s Congress, where he pushed the maximalist line on the South China Sea.

Mingyang supplied the turbines for a small project off Italy in 2022, and a smaller Shanghai company has since supplied an onshore farm in Croatia. That is the sum total so far of the Chinese presence in Europe’s wind industry.

“Wind is the last strategic clean tech sector with a truly European footprint,” said Giles Dickson, head of WindEurope.

Dickson said there is not a single Chinese turbine, nacelle or blade installed to date in Britain or anywhere in North Sea waters. In my view there is no necessary reason why any should be installed.

To argue that the UK’s existing wind infrastructure already poses a risk to national security is a stretch. The cables are vulnerable to Russian and Chinese sabotage but so are the much longer gas pipelines to Norway or to the UK’s North Sea fields.

So are the electricity interconnectors to French nuclear power, and so are data cables in the Irish Sea. This sort of risk – and it is very serious – is energy neutral.

There are Chinese rare earth magnets, gear boxes and other components in the wind supply chain – a dependency now being tackled with some urgency.

It is hard to see why this legacy vulnerability is any more serious than the thousands of Chinese large power transformers in the US grid or the near monopoly held by China’s ZPCM over ship-to-shore cranes used in US ports – so less hectoring from Donald Trump would be in order.

We navigate the risk of backdoors and kill switches as best we can with the sort of “layered defence” already used in aviation, military installations and nuclear power plants.

The larger point is that a component is not the same as an entire offshore wind turbine covered with data sensors, which are needed to manage these marvels of modern engineering.

If it were true that China could slash our wind energy costs in the same way that it has slashed costs for solar panels and electric vehicles, there might be a case for letting the fox further into the hen house. But it is not true.

“There is a great deal of exaggeration about the cost advantage of bringing in Chinese players,” said Anders Hove, from the Oxford Institute for Energy Studies (OIES).

The standard measure suggests that Europe’s offshore wind is twice as expensive – $3,389 (£2,544) per kilowatt (KW) against $1,520 per KW in China – but this mixes apples and oranges.

The capacity factor of Chinese turbines is around 37pc compared to 52pc or even higher for the latest European models on the Dogger Bank.

You cannot ship vast blades from China to Europe at viable cost. The rotor diameter for Mingyang’s 18 MW turbines is 920 feet. You would need an aircraft carrier to move the beast, if you could insure it at all.

The heavy parts such as the towers, foundations and blades would have to be manufactured in Europe, under European labour and energy costs. Skilled European crews would be needed to install them offshore. China’s cost advantage would largely disappear.

Europe as a whole has the manufacturing capacity to cover every turbine that it is practically able to deploy for at least another decade, whatever the pietistic targets. That includes the Siemens Gamesa blade plant in Hull and the Vestas plant in the Isle of Wight.

WindEurope says the region can produce 32 gigawatts (GW) each year but is installing only 20 GW annually today. The bottleneck is not a lack of turbines.

It is the unfathomable failure of the planning authorities in Britain, Germany and much of Europe to modernise the grid fast enough to keep pace with known renewable expansion plans.

The four new interconnectors bringing wind power from Scotland to the cities of England will not start to come on stream until the end of the decade, and the last two not until 2033.

In the meantime, the UK will soon be paying over £2bn a year to curtail excess wind that cannot be absorbed.

Mingyang wishes to build a £1.5bn plant at Ardersier Port in Scotland to build turbines and blades for the European market, a project still subject to a national security review. It is ardently supported by the Scottish Government, and as a general rule we should defer to Holyrood in such matters.

Octopus has offered a compromise that marries its software and data control with Mingyang’s hardware.

This does not solve the strategic/espionage threat because China retains a stranglehold of the supply of parts and still requires some access to control rooms. Beijing has shown that it will not hesitate to use such leverage.

The optimistic half of me would like to see the Ardersier plan go ahead as a one-off venture, with eyes wide open and under the strictest surveillance, for the sake of Union cohesion.

But a stronger voice says China’s behaviour has been so relentlessly hostile that we must draw a line in the sand.

China was welcomed into Western markets after Deng Xiaoping’s “opening up”. There was no concerted attempt to hold the country back. The democracies tolerated the “China Shock 1.0” that did so much harm to their own blue-collar workers.

China has abused this patience and goodwill. It ripped off the mask of “bide and hide” as soon as it calculated – prematurely, history will judge – that the West was weak enough to defeat.

It is Xi Jinping who has chosen to unleash a wolf warrior China, not the other way round, and he is actively helping to sustain a proxy war in Ukraine that aims to bring Europe to its knees.

China has weaponised critical minerals as a tool of coercion against all of the liberal democracies. It has imposed swingeing curbs on the export of materials and machinery used to make magnets or lithium batteries, or to recycle rare earths, or needed for quantum tech. Chinese flagged ships have twice dragged anchors over sub-sea cables in the Baltic.

The 15th Five-Year Plan from 2026-2030 persists with a model of massive over-production that long ago destabilised the global trading system and continues to get worse.

The country’s trade surplus hit a record $1.08tn for the year through November, and an ever bigger “China Shock 2.0” is landing on our doorstep in Europe.

The politburo seems to think it can keep doing this to us with impunity. It is time that we started erecting some barricades.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: For Britain to recover, the public sector needs shredding
data.description:
If we want a more prosperous and happier country, we have to make a major cultural and political shift: we must focus on creation rather than redistribution.

The Government, the media, and too many among the elite think exclusively about spending rather than growth. They all appear to see society as a zero sum equation: everything is about dividing up a fixed cake, rather than making the cake bigger.

Unfortunately 90 per cent of Labour’s MPs have never worked in the private sector, so they have no idea how wealth and progress are actually generated in the real world. They do not understand that only a powerful mixture of markets, competition, innovation, capital and talent can deliver rising living standards.

Britain is far too obsessed with public spending, taxes, welfare, benefits and the mechanics of government. The 21st-century ruling classes and a great many citizens are experts at entitlements and the workings of the state. But they are woefully ignorant of gritty stuff like commerce, manufacturing and technology.

We can only reverse the current spiral of ever higher taxation, welfare spending, debt and unemployment by embracing industry, work and enterprise. We need to renew Britain’s animal spirits.

It is almost laughable that Labour claimed in their manifesto that “their number one priority is growth”. Meanwhile they have doubled down on economically suicidal net-zero energy policies, which mean British industry has the highest electricity costs of any developed economy. This is completely crushing energy-intensive sectors like chemicals and metals.

They are proceeding remorselessly with the Employment Rights Bill, a 300-page burden seemingly designed to discourage every entrepreneur from ever hiring anyone. To pass such a destructive piece of legislation in the teeth of falling vacancies and rising joblessness demonstrates a political party in deep denial about our present circumstances. Yet it is set to be on the statute books shortly.

I am astonished at how expert so many people are about the demented bureaucracy of benefits and taxes. Both systems are an ever more complex game where citizens try to maximise their entitlements and minimise their taxes, and the authorities do the reverse. But none of this furious activity produces anything useful like goods or services, despite the vast amount of attention and time it absorbs. We have the longest tax code in the world: we should be ashamed of this dubious honour.

Research shows that newer companies tend to create most jobs, inventions and value added. Unfortunately too many hungry entrepreneurs are leaving to build their fortunes in places like America, Dubai and Australia. High taxes, onerous regulation, a socialist mindset and an all pervading sense of pessimism are driving them away. Corporate dissolutions have been sharply rising for the last five years, while company incorporations have slumped in the last three years, evidence that startup activity is in retreat.

The challenges facing the country are considerable: we have an ageing population, almost £3tn of national debt, creaking infrastructure, and face far more ferocious global competition than ever before. We have been living beyond our means for some time, and unless we want to embrace decline and immiseration, a major revolution in our national attitude is vital.

I suspect that our cabinet, MPs, civil servants, mayors and other figures in power have simply no comprehension of the economic upheaval we face. From AI to a rapidly rising Asia, the current model is broken: the metaphorical enemy are at the gates. To prevent Britain becoming a bigger version of Argentina – a tragic century of retreat – we require a Herculean effort. It demands a completely new set of priorities, many painful trade-offs, and a realisation that the management of decline is the worst betrayal. The dependency mentality must be junked. We must rediscover our ambition and capacity for hard work.

Instead of an overwhelming fixation with public spending and the state, the country needs to concentrate on retaining the proper means of production. If we cannot make anything, we become completely dependent on countries like China, the emerging workshop of the world.

This is not a wise or a safe arrangement. Without the means of production, we have no ability to defend ourselves. We are beggaring ourselves to enable many millions to avoid work, and pursuing an energy policy of virtue signalling while demolishing our industrial capacity. These are the politics of a madhouse, and we must change course urgently. Otherwise, in the decades to come, our children and grandchildren will curse us.

data.title: Canary Wharf revival gathers pace as Visa prepares to relocate European HQ
data.description:
Visa has struck a deal to move its European headquarters to London’s Canary Wharf, handing a fresh boost to the city’s financial district.

The US payments company has agreed to lease 300,000 sq ft on a 15-year deal at One Canada Square, relocating from its current base in Paddington in the summer of 2028.

The agreement is a boost for the Canary Wharf Group, which has been battling to regain its status after an exodus of companies in recent years.

A revival of the business district has been gathering momentum in recent weeks, with JP Morgan recently announcing plans to build a £3bn new headquarters there.

The US banking giant said it would build a three million sq ft office that will hold 12,000 people and serve as its main European base.

Shobi Khan, chief executive of Canary Wharf Group, said Visa’s move will mean the firm “joins a fintech community of more than 65 companies”.

It marks a reversal of fortunes for Canary Wharf after the likes of ratings agency Moody’s and magic circle law firm Clifford Chance ditched the district in favour of moves to the City of London.

However, the new string of deals shows the trend might be easing.

In August, Spanish lender BBVA signed a lease to take 250,000 sq ft in Canary Wharf, while Hershey’s, the American chocolate manufacturer, and Zopa, the digital bank, moved to the financial district this year.

Canary Wharf Group, which is co-owned by the Qatar Investment Authority and Canadian private equity firm Brookfield, said more than 750,000 sq ft of office transactions had been announced so far this year, making 2025 its most successful year in more than a decade.

In an attempt to make the district more attractive to potential tenants, Canary Wharf Group has expanded the area’s retail, leisure and hospitality offering. It has also increased the number of residential units and hotels in the area.

Mr Khan said Canary Wharf had been evolving to become “a thriving mixed-use district that supports customers at every stage of their journey”.

data.title: Jamie Dimon tells Trump Media boss ‘stop making up things’
data.description:
Jamie Dimon has attacked the chief executive of Trump Media over a brewing debanking row, telling him to “stop making up things”.

The boss of JP Morgan dismissed claims that the Wall Street bank had “debanked” the US president’s media company, insisting it did not remove accounts based on “religious or political affiliations”.

It comes after Devin Nunes, a former Republican congressman who now heads Trump Media, claimed JP Morgan had shut down the company’s accounts following pressure by the Biden administration.

In an interview on Fox News, Mr Dimon said: “People have to grow up here, okay? And stop making up things, and stuff like that.

“I can’t talk about an individual account. We do not debank people’s religious or political affiliations.

“We debank people who are Democrats. We debank people who are Republicans. We’ve debanked different religious folks, never was for that reason.”

Last month Mr Nunes attacked JP Morgan for cooperating with federal prosecutors investigating efforts to overturn the 2020 election.

Mr Nunes said at the time: “What reason do they have to go after us? I have seen Jamie Dimon and others at JP Morgan say this over and over again that they don’t debank people for political reasons. Well, then why the hell did you debank Trump Media?”

Mr Dimon insisted that the bank’s actions were governed strictly by law and not ideology.

He added that he considered the rules around reporting activity of bank’s clients to the government and closing accounts to be overly restrictive, and praised the efforts of the Trump administration to change the rules.

Mr Dimon said: “I actually applaud the Trump administration, who’s trying to say that debanking is bad and we should change the rules.

“I’ve been asking to change the rules now for 15 years ... That’s why a lot of stuff takes place and it’s really customer-unfriendly and we’re debanking people because of suspected things or negative media ... Let’s take a deep breath and fix the problem as opposed to blaming someone who is put in that position.”

JP Morgan would never provide information to the government just because they asked, Mr Dimon said, but added that if subpoenaed it was required to do so by law.

He said that he had managed subpoenas from the last four administrations, both under Democratic and Republican administrations.

There has been growing scrutiny of debanking after it emerged that Coutts, which is owned by NatWest, in 2023 closed Nigel Farage’s account amid row over his political views.

Dame Alison Rose, the chief executive of NatWest, and Peter Flavel, the chief executive of Coutts, were both ultimately forced to resign following the dispute.

In April next year, new rules will come into force in the UK which will force banks and other payment providers to give account holders a minimum of 90 days’ written notice before terminating the account and provide specific reasons for the closure.

According to the Financial Conduct Authority, 408,000 customers in the UK had their bank accounts shut down in 2023, compared to just 45,091 in 2016.

data.title: Leon to shut 20 stores and cut jobs as losses hit £10m
data.description:
Leon is preparing to shut 20 restaurants and cut jobs as its co-founder scrambles to turn around the business.

John Vincent, who repurchased the fast food chain in October, said Leon would reduce its estate from 70 to 50 locations across the country.

The struggling chain has hired administrators to oversee a Company Voluntary Arrangement (CVA), a legally sanctioned restructuring that will allow the business to push through closures.

Mr Vincent blamed high taxes and mismanagement under brothers Mohsin and Zuber Issa, the former owners, for the chain’s poor state. Leon has “drifted from some of its core principles” and is now “very different from the [company] I left”, Mr Vincent said in an email to customers.

He wrote: “The company is now losing money – around £10m a year – and too many of our restaurants are unprofitable.

“Partly this is the result of a change in culture within the company and partly it is due to external pressures felt by many restaurants in the UK. Fewer people in city centres, changes in working practices, rising costs and a tax system that makes it harder for the hospitality sector to be sustainable.”

Mr Vincent used the announcement to lobby the Government for tax cuts for the hospitality industry.

He said: “Today, for every pound we receive from the customer, around 36p goes to the Government in tax and about 2p ends up in the hands of the company. It’s why most players are reporting big losses.”

Mr Vincent co-founded Leon in 2004 with Henry Dimbleby and Allegra McEvedy. It was set up as a healthier alternative to fast food. Its success led the government to task Mr Dimbleby with leading a national review of the food system in 2019.

The business was sold to petrol forecourt giant EG Group, run by the Issas, in 2021. EG Group sold Leon to sister company Asda two years later as part of a broader deal. The supermarket was also co-owned by the Issas at that time.

In a statement, Mr Vincent said: “In the last two years, Asda had bigger fish to fry and Leon was always a business they didn’t feel fitted their strategy.

“If you look at the performance of Leon’s peers, you will see that everyone is facing challenges – companies are reporting significant losses due to working patterns and increasingly unsustainable taxes.”

The chain lost £8.83m in 2024 and sales fell from £64.9m to £62.5m. When Mr Vincent bought the business back, he promised to “bring Leon back to its original purpose and remove all the parts that don’t feel like Leon any more”.

As part of the restructuring, Leon has appointed Quantuma to advise on how to cut costs. The company said this “will involve ... a number of job losses”.

Affected staff will be relocated to other outlets where possible or encouraged to apply for a position at Pret A Manger “via a dedicated channel”, the company added.

A Quantuma spokesman said: “We are looking forward to working with the Leon team to deliver the optimal outcome for all stakeholders of the business, the creditors, suppliers and employees of the company.

“Leon is obviously a much-loved and cherished member of the retail food community, as we have already had very positive support from its supplier base and many of its landlords.”

data.title: Chocolate prices jump in run-up to Christmas
data.description:
Chocolate prices have jumped by almost a fifth in the run-up to Christmas, as a boom in cocoa supplies is failing to drive down costs.

New figures from Worldpanel revealed that chocolate prices are up 18.4pc on last year, leading to shoppers paying more for sweet treats at the supermarket.

The price of chocolate is rising faster than almost all other supermarket food items, according to Worldpanel, apart from fresh meat and chicken.

This has led to an increase in the price of well-known products, such as Cadbury’s bars, KitKats, Terry’s Chocolate Oranges and Galaxy chocolate.

KitKats are as much as 70pc more expensive than last year, while a 310g pack of Maltesers is £1.67 more costly, according to data from Trolley.co.uk, which tracks supermarket prices.

A spokesman for Mars Wrigley UK and Ireland, which makes Maltesers, said: “We know that the cost of living is front and centre for shoppers, which is why we remain focused on providing, high-quality products at the best possible value for money.

“We will always absorb pricing pressures where we can, but rising manufacturing costs – driven in part by well-documented increases in the cost of cocoa – have meant that we’ve had to adjust some of our product sizes.

“It’s not a decision we take lightly, but it ensures consumers can still enjoy their favourite Maltesers without compromising on quality or taste.”

The price of a Terry’s Chocolate Orange has also jumped by 38p.

Chocolate maker Whitakers, based in Skipton, North Yorkshire, said it had also been forced to increase prices by around a fifth on average since the start of the year, in part owing to the impact of tax rises under Labour.

William Whitaker, the managing director, said: “To have to go to your customer base and say, ‘I’m sorry, but on top of the National Insurance, the minimum wage, the business rates, everything else, we’ve got a 20pc cost rise,’ that really tests the relevance of your products.”

Less cocoa

Some big brands have already resorted to using less cocoa to try to offset the price increase.

In October, it emerged that McVitie’s Penguin and Club bars could no longer be classed as chocolate because of less cocoa being used in their recipes.

It follows years of soaring cocoa prices because of poor harvests in West Africa, including Ghana and the Ivory Coast.

Cocoa is currently priced at around $5,930 (£4,450) per tonne, up from around $2,500 before the pandemic.

Prices initially started to surge when countries emerged from Covid, as demand for chocolate outstripped supply.

Economists at ING said the 2024-25 trading season “finished with a surplus of around 60,000 tonnes, which would be the first surplus since the 2020-21 marketing year”.

They said next year was also likely to see surplus supply, helping to bring down global cocoa prices.

However, this drop is expected to take some time to filter across into cheaper chocolate for British customers, given that companies buy cocoa in advance.

Mr Whitaker said he did not expect many companies to lower prices for customers until Christmas next year at the earliest.

He added: “It depends on when chocolate is bought. For prices to be lower next Christmas, suppliers would have to be free of contracts and buy that chocolate today.”

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: What is a cash Isa, and how does one work?
data.description:
If you have money to save, then a cash Isa should be one of your considerations. It works in a similar way to a savings account, except that you’re limited to how much you can pay in each year, and the interest your money earns will remain tax-free.

Some changes are coming for cash Isa savers in April 2027. Under government plans announced in the 2025 autumn Budget, only over-65s will be able to continue saving up to £20,000 of their Isa allowance into a cash account. Younger savers will have their cash Isa limit reduced to £12,000, and the remaining £8,000 must be invested instead.

There are several types of cash Isas to choose from, and they’re offered by lots of banks and building societies, as well as National Savings & Investments.

If you’re not sure quite where to start, Telegraph Money will explain the basics of cash Isas in this guide, which will cover:

  • What is a cash Isa?
  • Benefits of a cash Isa
  • Types of cash Isas available
  • How to make cash Isas work for you
  • Cash Isa FAQs

What is a cash Isa?

A cash Isa is an individual savings account that allows you to earn interest on your savings without paying tax on the interest earned.

Cash Isas were launched in 1999 with the aim of helping savers reduce their tax bills.

While they went out of fashion when savings rates were at record lows – as there was little risk of savers earning enough in interest to trigger a charge – the higher rates we’ve seen over the last couple of years mean they have a more important role to play.

Savings rates reached upwards of 6pc in 2023, and it’s still possible to find deals above 4.5pc now, which means more people are breaching their “personal savings allowance” (more on this below), and are therefore at risk of having to pay tax on their interest.

If you’re trying to keep some of your cash out of the taxman’s clutches – not just now, but years in the future – saving tax-free in an Isa is a sensible move.

Who can open a cash Isa?

In order to open a cash Isa in the UK, you must:

  • Be aged 18 or over,
  • Be a UK resident for tax purposes, including Crown servants and their spouses serving overseas,
  • Have a National Insurance number, which is needed to apply for an Isa.

You can also open a junior Isa for your children if they are under 18. Discover the best junior cash Isa deals and rates in our dedicated article.

Benefits of a cash Isa

There are some key benefits to a cash Isa over a regular savings account:

  • Cash Isas currently allow you to pay in up to £20,000 a year, and earn interest free of income tax
  • They are separate from the Personal Savings Allowance (PSA)
  • Savings held within a cash Isa are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) up to £85,000
  • Cash Isas are flexible and easy to manage.

By sheltering money in a cash Isa you can keep more of the interest you earn in your own hands – and cash Isas are tax-free for life. As your savings grow, building with compound interest, an Isa means you can shelter more and more interest from the tax man.”

What’s more, cash Isas are easy to set up, and simple to operate.

Laith Khalaf, from AJ Bell, said: “These vehicles are good for storing money that you might need at the drop of a hat. Unlike a stocks and shares Isa, the value of your cash Isa will never fall.”

In this article, we focus on cash Isas, but you can read our comprehensive guide on how Isas work for more information about other account types.

Types of cash Isa

There are several different types of cash Isa on the market, including:

  • Fixed-rate cash Isas
  • Variable-rate cash Isas
  • Notice cash Isas
  • Regular cash Isas
  • Lifetime Isas
  • Junior cash Isas

Fixed-rate cash Isas

If you are happy to lock your money away, better rates are likely to be available on fixed-rate Isas, compared to variable cash Isas. However, they require you to commit to locking your money away for a set period of time, most commonly between one and five years.

If you need to withdraw your money before the end of the fixed term you’re likely to face a penalty – often, providers will charge you a certain amount of interest on the sum you’re withdrawing.

Variable-rate cash Isas

One common type on variable-rate account is an “easy-access” Isa – which usually allows you to get your hands on your money whenever you need it, penalty-free. That being said, individual accounts may have their own more restrictive withdrawal rules.

The main drawback to this kind of cash Isa is that rates can change at any time – so the rate of interest you sign up for could reduce in future. 

You also need to keep a close eye for any bonus rates, which may bump up the interest rate when you first open the account only to drop some time afterwards. If there is one, make a note to move your money to a more competitive deal once the bonus ends.

Notice cash Isas

Isas with a notice period tend to have slightly better rates than easy-access accounts (but this isn’t always the case). In theory, the longer the notice period, the higher the rate of interest is likely to be.

Be aware that with this type of Isa you can usually make unlimited withdrawals, but you must give a certain number of days’ notice before you can withdraw your funds. This can vary, but is most commonly between 30 days to 180 days. You need to bear this in mind if you’re likely to need faster access to your cash.

Regular cash Isas

Specific terms will vary, but these accounts commonly have a set amount that you can pay in each month – sometimes a certain number of deposits are required in order to get the advertised interest rate, such as 10 per year, or one every calendar month. You might be able to make withdrawals, but those offering a higher rate might not allow them for a set period.

Note that the advertised interest rate might sound high, but as you’re usually restricted to gradually paying in relatively low amounts, the actual interest you’ll earn will be fairly low.

Lifetime Isas

Technically, lifetime Isas are their own separate type of Isa – they have a different deposit limit (£4,000), and the money is meant to fund either your first home or retirement. However, we are mentioning them here since there are cash Isa options. You can find out more in our guide to Lisas.

Junior cash Isas

For children under the age of 18, parents or guardians can open a Junior Isa for them – there are cash and stocks and shares Isas available.

How to make cash Isas work for you

There are several steps to making the most of a cash Isa:

1. Work out if a cash Isa is the best option

There are certain savers who would do well to consider a cash Isa – usually those who need to retain access to their money. Mr Khalaf said: “Cash Isas are well suited to those building up a rainy day fund, and those saving for short-term spending needs – with the money needed in the next five years or so.”

2. Decide which type suits you

The key for this decision is working out how much flexibility you’ll need from your account – can you leave the money untouched for a year or two, or do you need to make withdrawals often? This can help you identify whether an easy-access Isa is best, or a fixed-term (which usually has higher rates). 

Of course, you don’t just have to choose one – you can split up your cash into different accounts, so you can retain access to some and lock up the rest. Our guide to the savings laddering tactic reveals how to split your savings to secure the highest rates year after year.

3. Find the best rates

With inflation remaining higher than the Bank of England’s 2pc target, price rises will be eroding the real terms buying power of your savings – therefore, it’s important to secure the best interest rate possible. 

Telegraph Money’s guide to the best cash Isas highlights the top rates across all account types, updated daily.

4. Act quickly to secure the best deals

When you’ve found a cash Isa that’s right for you it’s worth getting your skates on, as the top deals may not hang around for long. Rachel Springall, finance expert at moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Offers can change quickly, so speed is crucial to grab a top rate.”

5. Regularly review your account

After tucking money away in a cash Isa it’s key to review it regularly to ensure it’s still paying a competitive rate – particularly if it has a variable rate that can change at any time.

If you’ve signed up to a fixed-rate deal, make a note of when it finishes and set about finding a new home for your money – otherwise, your provider may move it to a low-rate easy-access account.

Understanding the tax benefits

One of the biggest benefits of a cash Isa is the fact that this vehicle protects your savings from tax.

As a saver, you need to take care not to breach your “personal savings allowance”. This is the limit on interest you can earn tax-free, and applies to the interest you earn across all accounts.

Your allowance depends on your tax band – i.e. the highest rate of income tax you pay. It is £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer, and just £500 for a higher-rate taxpayer. Additional-rate taxpayers pay tax on all their interest.

A consequence of income tax thresholds being frozen until at least 2030-31, as announced in the latest Budget, more people will be dragged into higher tax bands, and will therefore be more likely to breach their personal savings allowance. This is when sheltering your hard-earned savings in a cash Isa becomes so much more important.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no rule about how much money you can hold in a cash Isa – the restriction is just on how much you can pay in each tax year.

Under current rules, the annual Isa allowance is £20,000. You can opt to put up to £20,000 in one Isa, or split it among several. This £20,000 allowance has not changed since 2017.

From April 2027, there are plans to restrict the cash Isa allowance to £12,000 for savers aged under 65. They will still have an overall £20,000 Isa allowance, but the remaining £8,000 will need to be invested, rather than saved in cash. Those aged over 65 won’t be affected by these changes.

Note that if you don’t use your Isa allowance, you lose it, as it cannot be carried over to the next tax year.

Yes. If you have a cash Isa, you are allowed to move your money between providers at any time – and you can also move it to a different type of Isa, as long as the provider accepts transfers. But you must follow the official Isa transfer rules to ensure the funds remain under the protection of the tax-free wrapper.

If you withdraw money from your cash Isa to your bank account before putting it into a new cash Isa, your hard-earned savings will lose their tax-free status while they’re not held in an Isa, and the deposit will also be deducted from your Isa allowance. Transferring existing Isa savings directly between providers won’t incur these problems.

Ms Springall said: “You must ensure you transfer the cash in accordance with the rules to keep its tax-free wrapper. But note that not every cash Isa will allow transfers in from cash Isas – or stocks and shares Isas.”

Be sure to check this ahead of making any move.

In any one tax year, you can make as many transfers as you wish. Also note that following new rules which kicked in from April 6 2024 you can now make partial transfers of Isas.

The Government has announced plans to restrict transfers from stocks and shares Isas to cash Isas from April 2027, in a bid to stop savers circumventing the reduced cash Isa limit – full details on this are yet to be announced.

Rules state that when you go overseas you are no longer allowed to pay into your Isa after the end of the tax year that you move.

Beyond this date, for tax purposes, you are no longer a British resident and will not be eligible for an Isa. The only exception is if you are a Crown employee working abroad, such as a diplomat, civil servant or member of the armed forces.

You need to let your Isa provider as soon as you stop being a British resident.

The good news is that even if you no longer live in the UK, any existing Isas you have will remain open and active and you will be able to go on benefiting from tax relief on any cash held in your accounts. You are also still allowed to transfer your Isa to another provider.

Note though that the tax rules may be different in the country you’ve moved to, and that you could be required to pay tax in your new country on the money earned from your Isa – you need to check this.

If you do return to the UK and become a British resident once again, you will be able to start paying into your Isa (subject to the annual £20,000 allowance).

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”

data.title: Mishal Husain: Some BBC board members are biased
data.description:
The BBC board is not impartial and some members fail to leave their opinions at the door, Mishal Husain has claimed.

Asked if the BBC was guilty of Left-wing bias, as some of its critics allege, the former presenter of Radio 4’s Today programme suggested to Radio Times that the opposite was true at board level.

She spoke out after Sir Robbie Gibb, a board member and former Tory spin doctor, was accused by BBC insiders of leading a Right-wing “coup” that led to the resignations of Tim Davie, the director-general, and Deborah Turness, the head of BBC News.

She said: “They [Mr Davie and Ms Turness] were dealing with a board that has individuals within it who are not impartial.

“I was always told as a BBC journalist you leave your opinions at the door. You do expect that from the board as well and I don’t think that happened,” said Husain, who recently left the corporation after 26 years.

Of the search for Mr Davie’s successor, she said: “It’s a brave person, whoever they are.”

Husain said she had contacted former colleagues to offer support over the BBC’s recent woes, which were prompted by The Telegraph publishing a leaked dossier setting out allegations of bias in its news and current affairs reporting.

Evidence in the dossier included Panorama editing a speech by Donald Trump to give the impression that he was directly inciting his followers to storm the Capitol in Washington DC.

“I’d have to be heartless not to be thinking about what it’s like,” Husain said of her ex-colleagues. “I saw quite a few serious issues and scandals play out from the inside, but none of them felt existential in the way this one does. It makes me worried for the future.”

Husain is not the first former BBC presenter to suggest Right-wing bias on the BBC board.

Emily Maitlis, who left Newsnight in 2022, has said of Sir Robbie, who spent two years as Theresa May’s director of communications: “How can somebody with so much political baggage call himself an impartiality tsar? It absolutely makes a mockery of the whole thing.”

Husain is now at Bloomberg, where she hosts a weekly podcast. One of her first interviewees was Nigel Farage, who responded to one of her questions about Reform’s relationship with Russia by saying: “Listen, love, you’re trying ever so hard.”

Asked if she was tempted to pull him up on his comment, Husain said: “No, because we would have stopped talking about Russia and we would have started talking about me and him, and I didn’t want that.”

The presenter also spoke about being a Muslim in the public eye, in a world “where prejudice, discrimination and hatred is rising”.

She recalled: “About eight years ago, there was a young Muslim journalist at the BBC who said to me, ‘I admire that you don’t hide that you’re Muslim,’ and I thought, how sad that he’s even saying that. That is one of the moments that brought home the sense of responsibility.”

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Banks in mortgage war as rates plunge
data.description:
Mortgage lenders have launched a pre-Christmas “rates war” ahead of an expected cut to the Bank Rate next week.

Both Natwest and Barclays cut their rates on Monday, with headline rates for the former dropping to as little as 3.62pc for a two-year fixed deal, or a 3.66pc “product transfer” for those borrowers looking to remortgage with the lender.

Barclays dropped rates on its two-year mortgages by as much as 0.10 percentage points to 3.70pc. The lender cut rates for its five-year fixes before last month’s Budget.

More than 20 banks cut their rates last week, including some of the largest lenders such as Santander and Nationwide.

Santander has launched rates of 3.51pc for those moving home – but the offer was only available on loans between £500,000 and £2m. Nationwide recently cut the interest on its deals to the lowest rate since 2022, with a 3.58pc two-year fix.

Rachel Springall, of analysts Moneyfacts, said: “Mortgage rates continue on the downward trend and November was particularly fruitful for fixed rate cuts.

“The re-pricing by lenders led to the average five-year fixed rate dropping below 5pc for the first time in over two years and sits at its lowest point since before the ‘mini-Budget’ in September 2022, alongside its two-year counterpart.”

Hina Bhudia, a partner at Knight Frank Finance, said: “The Budget removed some uncertainty over the path of borrowing costs, which is giving lenders the scope to keep trimming mortgage rates.

“This bodes well for 2026. Purchasing activity is running in line with 2019 levels despite this period of fiscal uncertainty, suggesting there is pent-up demand among purchasers. We should see that released as mortgage rates drift lower and we move into the new year.”

Jack Tutton, of brokers SJ Mortgages, said: “The market is hotting up due to the strong prediction of a Bank Rate cut before Christmas and potentially more cuts to come in 2026.

“Mortgage holders will be hoping that these cuts are a gift that keeps on giving and more reductions from more lenders will follow.”

Mortgage rates surged in the wake of Liz Truss’s disastrous “mini-Budget” in 2022, having been at record low levels during the pandemic. By August 2023, the average five-year fix had hit 6.85pc, while the average two-year deal peaked at 6.22pc.

But the average two-year fixed mortgage rate on Monday was 4.85pc, according to Moneyfacts. The average five-year deal was slightly higher, at 4.91pc. The average two-year tracker rate was 4.66pc.

The Bank Rate is widely expected to fall from its current rate of 4pc to 3.75pc at a meeting of the Monetary Policy Committee next Thursday. There have been three cuts so far this year, with more priced in for next year.

Inflation fell to 3.6pc in October, from 3.8pc, which gave the central bank more room to cut the official interest rate, and meant mortgage lenders began to cut their rates.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: Workers’ rights reforms ‘will force companies to hire even more HR staff’
data.description:
Labour’s workers’ rights overhaul will introduce “unlimited red tape” and force companies to hire even more HR staff, the boss of a British airline has said.

Luke Farajallah, the chief executive of regional carrier Loganair, said the reforms will increase bureaucracy and expenses, putting pressure on businesses already grappling with high taxes.

The Government’s own estimates suggest the employment rights bill – which was championed by Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister – will mean £5bn of increased costs for companies.

Mr Farajallah said: “There is going to be an unlimited amount of red tape to manage in our business because of that bill. It doesn’t make Loganair a bad employer because we’re against it.

“It makes us sensible in not wanting to shell out more money to have more process, more lawyers, more litigation, more policies and more HR departments than we need.”

Labour’s planned workers’ rights changes will allow workers to sue for unfair dismissal after six months rather than two years and will empower unions in the workplace, among other changes. Business groups have roundly criticised the changes, warning they will do little to help workers but will instead create more litigation and friction.

Loganair is one of Britain’s last remaining regional airlines – carriers that generally link secondary cities via relatively short routes. The sector is already under pressure with two regional airlines recently going bust.

Mr Farajallah said the workers’ rights changes would ramp up cost pressures on already-stretched domestic flights.

He said smaller airlines in particular were already reeling from Labour’s increase in air passenger duty, which will hit in April, and a requirement for them to move to a 10pc blend of sustainable aviation fuel “five times more expensive than it is today”.

The decision by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to lift National Insurance contributions for employers, meanwhile, added £800,000 to the Loganair wage bill “that could have gone back into the business”.

Mr Farajallah said: “The reality is that legislation is adding a huge burden to us as a business before we get out of bed in the morning. We’re fighting to stand still a lot of the time.

“We strive to be as low-cost as we can – but the truth is, there is a cost to flying regionally.”

Two of Britain’s last remaining regional airlines have folded since the start of November. Eastern Airways, based at Humberside Airport, went into administration on Nov 6. Blue Islands, which served Jersey and Guernsey, entered liquidation 10 days later. Both had been operating for almost three decades.

Even before the failures, the number of domestic UK flights had dropped 53pc in 20 years, according to aviation data firm Cirium. The 2020 collapse of Flybe, once Europe’s biggest regional airline, marked the biggest retreat.

While Glasgow-based Loganair has been able to boost passenger numbers by expanding across most of Britain, Mr Farajallah warned that the decline of the offshore oil industry amid the imposition of windfall taxes was hastening the demise of regional flights.

He said: “The routes vacated by Eastern – Teesside, Humberside, Newcastle – were generally up and down to Aberdeen and fed the oil and gas industry.

“But we are seeing almost no new projects, almost no exploration. Everything is renewables which means different people, different locations.

“We would love to put on scheduled transport up and down that eastern seaboard and we have had conversations with all of the airports but it doesn’t make economic sense.”

Mr Farajallah added that many people on Scottish islands are now taking the ferry because airfares are too high.

He warned that government backing for the expansion of airports including Heathrow and Gatwick will be pointless if more carriers are extinguished before the planned new runways are operating.

The Loganair chief said: “It doesn’t make any sense if you’re adding infrastructure and you haven’t got airlines to fly to that infrastructure.”

data.title: Ed Miliband attacks ‘ignorant’ SNP for blocking nuclear power
data.description:
Ed Miliband has attacked SNP leaders’ “total ignorance” in banning new nuclear power plants in Scotland as he urged communities to summon their “inner Yimby”.

The Energy Secretary said on Thursday there should be a debate about the Scottish Government’s existing policy, which he argued was stopping the country from sharing in the creation of jobs and prosperity.

A UK-wide search is under way for sites that can host large-scale nuclear power stations, with Mr Miliband ordering officials to include Scotland – despite the ban imposed by the SNP-run devolved administration.

His comments come as the parties gear up for the Scottish parliamentary elections in May. Labour is currently trailing the SNP, the Greens and the Liberal Democrats in the polls.

Mr Miliband told a nuclear industry event in London: “We need to have the discussion. And I’m sure the Scottish election will frame a discussion on this, which is, ‘Why should Scotland be excluded from the benefits of new nuclear?’

“It makes no sense for Scotland, it makes no sense for the United Kingdom as a whole.

“In the end, these are going to be decisions for a Scottish Government. But we want to maximise the opportunities for the whole of the United Kingdom.

“So why would we turn our backs on an industry – where, right across the world, people are embracing it – for reasons, frankly, of total dogma and total ignorance?”

There are more than 98,000 jobs in the UK’s civil nuclear supply chain, including power station operators, construction firms, fuels, decommissioning and waste management, according to the Nuclear Industry Association (NIA).

Around 5,400 of these jobs are in Scotland.

But with Torness, the country’s last remaining nuclear power station, scheduled to close by the end of this decade, that figure is expected to dwindle over time as efforts switch to decommissioning.

By comparison, two new large-scale plants are under construction in England – Hinkley Point C in Somerset and Sizewell C in Suffolk – with the Government also preparing to build the first small modular reactors (SMRs) with Rolls-Royce on the island of Anglesey in North Wales.

At the peak of construction, the SMR development alone is expected to create up to 3,000 jobs.

The siting review for large nuclear plants is expected to report back in the second half of next year.

Mr Miliband’s remarks follow the publication of a landmark report led by John Fingleton, an economist, which recommended “radical” reforms to speed up the process of approving and building plants.

In his speech, the Energy Secretary hailed the proposals and said the Government believed that “you can’t have energy sovereignty in abundance without making it easier to build things in this country”.

He added: “This Government is willing to have that discussion with the public. We’re willing to have the discussion across renewables, and we’re willing to have it in nuclear as well.

“The need for homegrown clean energy brings out one’s inner Yimby [yes in my backyard]. I can feel my inner Yimby coming out.

“We shouldn’t accept the stagnation and delay that is all too often built into our planning system.”

He also cleared the way for a new wave of privately funded nuclear projects to come forward, promising that the Government would shortly publish guidance for how these could be supported.

Centrica, the British Gas owner, and Amazon-backed X-Energy are among those vying to build their own fleets of mini reactors across Britain, underpinned by a deal between Sir Keir Starmer and Donald Trump that aims to boost transatlantic cooperation on design approvals.

The companies and others are pushing for a relaxation of siting rules to allow construction closer to built-up areas, as well as potentially contentious proposals that could see consumers fund the plants upfront through their bills.

Some nuclear developers including Newcleo have previously scrapped plans for Britain after warnings that the Government was taking too long to set out a path for such private projects.

However, Mr Miliband insisted: “We are really serious about these privately led routes to market, and that’s what our framework will provide.

“Speed really matters. We don’t want to hang around. There is a global race on, and we want to win the global race.”

Gillian Martin, the Scottish energy secretary, said: “The Scottish Government is focussed on supporting growth and creating jobs by capitalising on Scotland’s immense renewable energy capacity rather than expensive new nuclear energy which takes decades to build and creates a legacy of radioactive waste.

“Significant growth in renewables is providing key opportunities for our future energy workforce in Scotland, with independent scenarios from Ernst and Young showing that with the right support, Scotland’s low carbon and renewable energy sector could support nearly 80,000 jobs by 2050.”

data.title: Nationwide hit with record fine after failing to spot customer’s £27m Covid fraud
data.description:
Nationwide has been hit with a record fine after failing to spot a customer’s £27.3m furlough fraud.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has ordered the British lender to pay £44m as a result of its failings, which included the incident during Covid.

The City watchdog revealed in a ruling on Friday that a Nationwide customer deposited £26m into their current account over the space of eight days, having received 24 furlough payments totalling £27.3m over 13 months.

HMRC was able to recover £26.5m from the fraud, but roughly £800,000 remains unaccounted for.

The FCA added that between October 2016 and July 2021, Nationwide had ineffective systems for keeping up to date with due diligence and risk assessments for all its personal current account customers.

The fine was announced just a day after Dame Debbie Crosbie, the chief executive of Nationwide, was appointed as the Government’s new Women in Finance champion.

As part of its findings, the FCA said the bank was aware of its customers using personal accounts for business transactions, which was a breach of its terms because it did not yet offer business accounts.

This left Nationwide unable to effectively monitor money-laundering risks, the regulator said.

Therese Chambers, of the FCA, said: “Nationwide failed to get a proper grip of the financial crime risks lurking within its customer base.

“It took too long to address its flawed systems and weak controls, meaning red flags were missed with serious consequences.

“Building societies and banks have a key role in the fight against financial crime. Firms must remain vigilant in this fight.”

A Nationwide spokesman said it had identified the issues through its own review and voluntarily brought them to the attention of the FCA.

The bank would have been fined almost £63m, but qualified for a 30pc discount after fully cooperating with the FCA and agreeing to resolve the issues. The £44m fine is the biggest Nationwide has ever received.

Nationwide said it has since invested significantly in its economic crime control framework, adding that it did not believe that the issues caused financial loss to any of its customers.

“We are sorry that our controls during the period fell below the high standards we expect,” the spokesman added.
